book.name: 1 Kings
book.author: Unknown (traditionally attributed to Jeremiah or a prophetic school; draws on court records, temple archives, and the 'Book of the Acts of Solomon')
book.dateWritten: c. 560–540 BC (final compilation during the Babylonian exile, using earlier sources)
book.writtenFrom: Babylon, using royal archives and prophetic records from Israel and Judah
book.writtenTo: The exiled community of Judah -- explaining how the nation went from Solomon's golden age to division, idolatry, and eventual destruction

explainer.overview: 1 Kings traces Israel's story from the death of David through the glorious reign of Solomon, the catastrophic division of the kingdom, and the reigns of the early kings of both Israel (north) and Judah (south). The book reaches its dramatic peak with Elijah's confrontation with the prophets of Baal on Mount Carmel. 1 Kings is fundamentally about the consequences of faithfulness and unfaithfulness to God's covenant -- Solomon's wisdom and wealth are gifts of obedience, while the kingdom's division is the price of idolatry.
explainer.historicalContext: The events of 1 Kings span roughly 970–850 BC. Solomon inherited David's empire at its peak and expanded it through diplomacy, trade, and massive building projects, including the temple in Jerusalem. After his death, oppressive taxation and forced labor caused the northern tribes to secede under Jeroboam, creating two rival kingdoms. The northern kingdom immediately fell into idolatry with golden calves at Dan and Bethel, while Judah's fidelity fluctuated between good and bad kings.
explainer.whyItMatters: 1 Kings establishes patterns that run through the rest of Israel's history: the temple as God's dwelling place, the Davidic covenant as the basis for Judah's survival, the prophetic voice as God's corrective, and the devastating consequences of idolatry. Solomon's temple dedication prayer (chapter 8) becomes the theological foundation for Israel's worship, while the kingdom's division demonstrates that no amount of wealth or wisdom can compensate for disobedience to God.
keyThemes: Wisdom as God's gift and its limits
The temple as God's dwelling among His people
The consequences of idolatry on national life
The division of the kingdom as divine judgment
The prophetic voice confronting royal power
The pattern of evaluating kings by their fidelity to Yahweh
God's sovereignty over the rise and fall of nations

people.name: Solomon
people.role: King of Israel, son of David and Bathsheba
people.bio: Solomon receives divine wisdom, builds the temple, and establishes Israel as a wealthy trading empire. His reign is the golden age of Israelite prosperity and cultural achievement. Yet his hundreds of foreign wives lead him into idolatry in his later years, and his oppressive taxation and forced labor programs create the grievances that split the kingdom after his death. Solomon embodies the paradox of wisdom without obedience -- the wisest man alive could not follow his own best counsel.
people.culturalContext: Solomon ruled during a continued power vacuum in the ancient Near East, allowing Israel to dominate regional trade routes. His alliance with Hiram of Tyre and marriage to Pharaoh's daughter placed Israel among the great powers. His building projects -- temple, palace, fortified cities -- transformed Israel from a tribal state into a sophisticated kingdom.

people.name: Elijah
people.role: Prophet to the northern kingdom of Israel
people.bio: Elijah appears without introduction or genealogy, erupting onto the scene to confront King Ahab and the Baal worship promoted by Queen Jezebel. He pronounces drought, is fed by ravens and a widow, raises the dead, confronts 450 prophets of Baal on Mount Carmel, and then flees in depression to Mount Horeb where God speaks in a still small voice. Elijah represents the prophetic tradition at its most dramatic -- one man standing against an entire corrupt system.
people.culturalContext: Elijah ministered during the reign of Ahab (c. 874-853 BC), when the northern kingdom had largely abandoned Yahweh worship in favor of Baal, the Canaanite storm god. Queen Jezebel, a Phoenician princess, had imported Baal worship as official state religion and was systematically eliminating Yahweh's prophets.

people.name: Rehoboam
people.role: Solomon's son, first king of the divided kingdom of Judah
people.bio: Rehoboam's arrogant rejection of the northern tribes' plea for tax relief triggers the permanent division of the kingdom. His foolish choice to listen to young advisors rather than experienced elders becomes the textbook example of leadership failure. He retains only Judah and Benjamin, while ten tribes follow Jeroboam.
people.culturalContext: Rehoboam's decision at Shechem reflects the tension between centralized monarchy and tribal autonomy that had existed since the beginning of Israel's monarchy. The northern tribes' grievances were legitimate -- Solomon's building projects had been funded by crushing taxation and forced labor.

people.name: Jeroboam I
people.role: First king of the northern kingdom of Israel
people.bio: Jeroboam was a capable administrator under Solomon who fled to Egypt after Ahijah the prophet promised him ten tribes. After the division, he became the prototype of a wicked king by establishing golden calf worship at Dan and Bethel to prevent his people from traveling to Jerusalem's temple. 'The sins of Jeroboam' becomes the recurring formula by which every subsequent northern king is condemned.
people.culturalContext: Jeroboam's golden calves at Dan and Bethel were politically motivated -- if his subjects worshiped at Jerusalem's temple, their loyalty might shift back to the Davidic dynasty. His religious innovation was political strategy masquerading as theology.

people.name: Ahab
people.role: King of the northern kingdom of Israel, husband of Jezebel
people.bio: Ahab is presented as the worst king in Israel's history -- not because he lacked ability (he was a skilled diplomat and warrior) but because he married Jezebel and promoted Baal worship as state religion. His reign brings Israel to its lowest spiritual point, provoking the dramatic ministry of Elijah. His personal weakness before Jezebel contrasts with his military boldness against Syria.
people.culturalContext: Ahab's marriage to Jezebel, princess of Sidon, was a diplomatic alliance that brought enormous wealth and strategic advantage but introduced systematic Baal worship into Israel. His ivory palace at Samaria, confirmed by archaeology, demonstrates his prosperity even as his spiritual legacy was disastrous.

people.name: Jezebel
people.role: Queen of Israel, wife of Ahab, daughter of the king of Sidon
people.bio: Jezebel is the most vilified woman in the Old Testament -- a Phoenician princess who imported Baal worship, murdered Yahweh's prophets, and engineered the judicial murder of Naboth to seize his vineyard for Ahab. She represents the danger of foreign religious influence and the abuse of royal power. Her name became synonymous with wicked female influence throughout subsequent biblical and Western tradition.
people.culturalContext: As daughter of Ethbaal, king of Sidon, Jezebel brought the sophisticated but morally corrupt Phoenician religious practices into Israel. Her 450 prophets of Baal and 400 prophets of Asherah ate at her table -- a massive religious establishment that rivaled and aimed to replace Yahweh worship.

places.name: Jerusalem / The Temple
places.description: Jerusalem remained the capital of the united kingdom under Solomon and then of Judah after the division. Solomon's temple, built on the threshing floor David purchased from Araunah, became the center of Israelite worship and the place where God's presence dwelt.
places.culturalSignificance: The temple was not merely a religious building but the cosmic center of Israel's world -- the place where heaven and earth intersected. Its destruction by Babylon in 586 BC was the defining catastrophe of Old Testament history.
places.modernLocation: Jerusalem, Israel/Palestine. The Temple Mount (Haram al-Sharif) is the traditional site.

places.name: Shechem
places.description: The ancient city where Rehoboam went to be confirmed as king by the northern tribes, and where the kingdom was permanently divided. Shechem had been a center of Israelite tribal assembly since the days of Joshua.
places.culturalSignificance: Shechem was the traditional gathering place of the northern tribes, associated with the covenant ceremonies of Joshua 24. Rehoboam's choice to meet the north at Shechem was politically necessary but ultimately the setting for national disaster.
places.modernLocation: Tell Balata, near modern Nablus, West Bank.

places.name: Mount Carmel
places.description: The dramatic headland where Elijah confronted 450 prophets of Baal in a contest of divine power. Fire fell from heaven consuming Elijah's sacrifice, and the prophets of Baal were executed at the Kishon brook below.
places.culturalSignificance: Mount Carmel was on the boundary between Israelite and Phoenician territory, making it symbolically appropriate for a contest between Yahweh and Baal. The mountain's association with fertility made it a natural site for Baal worship.

places.name: Samaria
places.description: The capital city built by Omri (Ahab's father) on a hill he purchased for the northern kingdom. Samaria replaced Tirzah as the capital and remained the political center of Israel until its destruction by Assyria in 722 BC.
places.culturalSignificance: Samaria was a purpose-built capital, strategically located and heavily fortified. Ahab's ivory palace there (confirmed by archaeology) symbolized the wealth and corruption of the northern kingdom. The city's name eventually became synonymous with the entire northern kingdom.
places.modernLocation: Sebastia, near modern Nablus, West Bank.

explainer.summary: David is old and feeble. His son Adonijah attempts to seize the throne, supported by Joab and Abiathar. Bathsheba and Nathan intervene, reminding David of his oath to make Solomon king. David orders Solomon's immediate anointing, which trumps Adonijah's premature celebration.
themes: Political intrigue in the royal court
explainer.contextInBook: The book opens with the unresolved question from 2 Samuel: who will succeed David? Adonijah's attempt mirrors Absalom's rebellion in method if not scale. Solomon's anointing fulfills God's designation (2 Samuel 12:24-25) and Nathan's name Jedidiah ('beloved of Yahweh').

wordStudies.translatedAs: Adonijah 'lifted himself up' -- the same self-prom
wordStudies.meaning: Adonijah 'lifted himself up' -- the same self-promotion language used of Absalom (2 Samuel 15:1). Both sons assumed they deserved the throne rather than waiting for God's appointment.

wordStudies.translatedAs: To pour oil on someone as a sign of divine selecti
wordStudies.meaning: To pour oil on someone as a sign of divine selection and empowerment. Solomon is 'anointed' (mashach), from which we get 'Messiah' (mashiach) -- the anointed one.

verses.text: Now king David was old and advanced in years; and they covered him with clothes, but he couldn’t keep warm.
verses.explainer: David is old, bedridden, and cannot stay warm -- a vivid picture of royal decline. The mighty warrior who once killed Goliath is now frail and dying.

annotations.word: king David was old and advanced in years

verses.text: Therefore his servants said to him, “Let a young virgin be sought for my lord the king. Let her stand before the king, and cherish him; and let her lie in your bosom, that my lord the king may keep warm.”
verses.explainer: David's servants suggest finding a young woman to serve as nurse and warm him. This was a recognized medical treatment in the ancient world, not primarily sexual.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: Using body heat as a medical treatment for the elderly was a recognized practice in the ancient Near East. The selection of a beautiful young woman also served a political purpose: if David could not function sexually, he was no longer fit to rule.

verses.text: So they sought for a beautiful young lady throughout all the borders of Israel, and found Abishag the Shunammite, and brought her to the king.
verses.explainer: Abishag the Shunammite is selected from a nationwide search. She is beautiful and serves David faithfully, but the narrator emphasizes: the king did not have sexual relations with her.

annotations.word: Abishag the Shunammite

verses.text: The young lady was very beautiful; and she cherished the king, and served him; but the king didn’t know her intimately.
verses.explainer: The narrator reiterates that David did not have sexual relations with Abishag. This detail becomes crucial for understanding the political crisis that follows.

verses.text: Then Adonijah the son of Haggith exalted himself, saying, “I will be king.” Then he prepared him chariots and horsemen, and fifty men to run before him.
verses.explainer: Adonijah, David's eldest surviving son, declares 'I will be king.' He prepares chariots, horses, and fifty runners -- the identical display Absalom used.

annotations.word: Adonijah the son of Haggith exalted himself
chariots and horsemen, and fifty men to run before him

crossReferences.connection: Absalom used the identical display of chariots and runners to claim royal status before his rebellion

verses.text: His father had not displeased him at any time in saying, “Why have you done so?” and he was also a very handsome man; and he was born after Absalom.
verses.explainer: The narrator reveals David never disciplined Adonijah, never asking 'Why have you done this?' -- the same parental failure that enabled Absalom.

verses.text: He conferred with Joab the son of Zeruiah, and with Abiathar the priest; and they followed Adonijah and helped him.
verses.explainer: Adonijah recruits Joab and Abiathar the priest -- powerful allies from the old guard. These veteran supporters give his claim institutional backing.

annotations.word: Joab the son of Zeruiah
Abiathar the priest

verses.text: But Zadok the priest, Benaiah the son of Jehoiada, Nathan the prophet, Shimei, Rei, and the mighty men who belonged to David, were not with Adonijah.
verses.explainer: The opposing faction: Zadok the priest, Benaiah the bodyguard commander, Nathan the prophet, and the mighty men support Solomon.

verses.text: Adonijah killed sheep, cattle, and fatlings by the stone of Zoheleth, which is beside En Rogel; and he called all his brothers, the king’s sons, and all the men of Judah, the king’s servants;
verses.explainer: Adonijah holds a coronation feast at the Stone of Zoheleth near En-rogel, inviting all his brothers and royal officials -- but pointedly excluding Solomon.

verses.text: but he didn’t call Nathan the prophet, and Benaiah, and the mighty men, and Solomon his brother.
verses.explainer: Solomon and Nathan are specifically not invited. The exclusion confirms that Adonijah views Solomon as his rival and Nathan as Solomon's advocate.

verses.text: Then Nathan spoke to Bathsheba the mother of Solomon, saying, “Haven’t you heard that Adonijah the son of Haggith reigns, and David our lord doesn’t know it?
verses.explainer: Nathan the prophet takes the initiative, approaching Bathsheba with news of Adonijah's attempted coup and a plan to secure Solomon's succession.

annotations.word: Nathan spoke to Bathsheba the mother of Solomon

verses.text: Now therefore come, please let me give you counsel, that you may save your own life, and your son Solomon’s life.
verses.explainer: Nathan urges Bathsheba to act immediately to save both her life and Solomon's. If Adonijah becomes king, Solomon's faction faces execution.

verses.text: Go in to king David, and tell him, ‘Didn’t you, my lord, king, swear to your servant, saying, “Assuredly Solomon your son shall reign after me, and he shall sit on my throne?” Why then does Adonijah reign?’
verses.explainer: Nathan scripts Bathsheba's approach: remind David of his oath that Solomon would succeed him. This oath is not recorded elsewhere but is presented as genuine.

verses.text: Behold, while you are still talking there with the king, I will also come in after you and confirm your words.”
verses.explainer: Nathan promises to come in during Bathsheba's audience with David and confirm her words. The coordinated approach ensures David cannot dismiss either appeal individually.

verses.text: Bathsheba went in to the king in his room. The king was very old; and Abishag the Shunammite was serving the king.
verses.explainer: Bathsheba enters David's chamber where Abishag is attending him. The scene emphasizes David's frailty -- he needs constant nursing care.

verses.text: Bathsheba bowed, and showed respect to the king. The king said, “What would you like?”
verses.explainer: Bathsheba bows and prostrates herself before David. Even as queen mother, she approaches the king with full formal respect.

annotations.word: Bathsheba bowed, and showed respect to the king

verses.text: She said to him, “My lord, you swore by Yahweh your God to your servant, ‘Assuredly Solomon your son shall reign after me, and he shall sit on my throne.’
verses.explainer: Bathsheba reminds David of his sworn oath that Solomon would be king. She frames the crisis as David's unfulfilled promise.

annotations.word: you swore by Yahweh your God to your servant

verses.text: Now, behold, Adonijah reigns; and you, my lord the king, don’t know it.
verses.explainer: Bathsheba reports Adonijah's coronation feast and tells David he does not know about it. She implies David is being bypassed while incapacitated.

verses.text: He has slain cattle and fatlings and sheep in abundance, and has called all the sons of the king, Abiathar the priest, and Joab the captain of the army; but he hasn’t called Solomon your servant.
verses.explainer: Bathsheba describes the scope of Adonijah's support: oxen, fatlings, sheep in abundance, plus Joab and Abiathar. The numbers suggest a full coronation ceremony.

verses.text: You, my lord the king, the eyes of all Israel are on you, that you should tell them who will sit on the throne of my lord the king after him.
verses.explainer: Bathsheba tells David that all Israel is watching him to see who he will name as successor. She places the responsibility squarely on the dying king.

annotations.word: the eyes of all Israel are on you

verses.text: Otherwise it will happen, when my lord the king sleeps with his fathers, that I and my son Solomon will be considered criminals.”
verses.explainer: Bathsheba adds the dire personal warning: if David dies without acting, she and Solomon will be counted as traitors and executed by the new regime.

verses.text: Behold, while she was still talking with the king, Nathan the prophet came in.
verses.explainer: Nathan arrives exactly as planned, during Bathsheba's audience. The timing is coordinated to maximize the persuasive impact on David.

verses.text:
They told the king, saying, “Behold, Nathan the prophet!”
When he had come in before the king, he bowed himself before the king with his face to the ground.
verses.explainer: Nathan prostrates himself before the king and asks whether David has designated Adonijah as his successor without informing his own court prophet.

verses.text: Nathan said, “My lord, king, have you said, ‘Adonijah shall reign after me, and he shall sit on my throne?’
verses.explainer: Nathan describes Adonijah's feast and supporters, adding that they are already crying 'Long live king Adonijah!' The crisis is immediate.

verses.text: For he has gone down today, and has slain cattle, fatlings, and sheep in abundance, and has called all the king’s sons, the captains of the army, and Abiathar the priest. Behold, they are eating and drinking before him, and saying, ‘Long live king Adonijah!’
verses.explainer: Nathan lists everyone Adonijah invited and notes that Solomon, Zadok, Benaiah, and he himself were excluded. The exclusion proves this is a factional coup, not a family celebration.

verses.text: But he hasn’t called me, even me your servant, Zadok the priest, Benaiah the son of Jehoiada, and your servant Solomon.
verses.explainer: Nathan asks David directly: 'Is this thing done by my lord the king, and you have not told your servant?' He forces David to choose.

verses.text: Was this thing done by my lord the king, and you haven’t shown to your servants who should sit on the throne of my lord the king after him?”
verses.explainer: David's response is immediate and decisive. He summons Bathsheba back (she had withdrawn) and reaffirms his oath.

verses.text: Then king David answered, “Call Bathsheba in to me.” She came into the king’s presence and stood before the king.
verses.explainer: David swears again by Yahweh: Solomon will sit on my throne today. The word 'today' transforms the oath from future intention to immediate action.

verses.text: The king swore, and said, “As Yahweh lives, who has redeemed my soul out of all adversity,
verses.explainer: David swears by the living Yahweh who has redeemed his soul from every adversity. His oath invokes God as witness and guarantor.

verses.text: most certainly as I swore to you by Yahweh, the God of Israel, saying, ‘Assuredly Solomon your son shall reign after me, and he shall sit on my throne in my place;’ I will most certainly do this today.”
verses.explainer: David quotes his original oath to Bathsheba verbatim: Solomon shall reign after me and sit on my throne in my place. He commits fully to Solomon's succession.

annotations.word: Solomon your son shall reign after me, and he shall sit on my throne in my place

verses.text: Then Bathsheba bowed with her face to the earth, and showed respect to the king, and said, “Let my lord king David live forever!”
verses.explainer: Bathsheba prostrates herself and says 'Let my lord king David live forever.' The formal response acknowledges the king's authority and expresses gratitude.

annotations.word: Let my lord king David live forever

verses.text: King David said, “Call to me Zadok the priest, Nathan the prophet, and Benaiah the son of Jehoiada.” They came before the king.
verses.explainer: David summons Zadok, Nathan, and Benaiah -- the priestly, prophetic, and military heads of Solomon's faction. The three pillars of legitimate authority will execute the anointing.

annotations.word: Zadok the priest, Nathan the prophet, and Benaiah

verses.text: The king said to them, “Take with you the servants of your lord, and cause Solomon my son to ride on my own mule, and bring him down to Gihon.
verses.explainer: David gives detailed instructions: put Solomon on the king's mule and bring him to Gihon spring. The royal mule is a symbol of succession -- only the designated heir rides it.

annotations.word: cause Solomon my son to ride on my own mule
Gihon

verses.text: Let Zadok the priest and Nathan the prophet anoint him there king over Israel. Blow the trumpet, and say, ‘Long live king Solomon!’
verses.explainer: Zadok and Nathan are to anoint Solomon there, then blow the trumpet and proclaim 'Long live king Solomon.' The ritual transfers authority publicly and irrevocably.

verses.text: Then come up after him, and he shall come and sit on my throne; for he shall be king in my place. I have appointed him to be prince over Israel and over Judah.”
verses.explainer: Solomon is to come back and sit on David's throne. David declares 'He shall be king in my place.' The succession is a living transfer, not a posthumous inheritance.

annotations.word: he shall be king in my place

verses.text: Benaiah the son of Jehoiada answered the king, and said, “Amen. May Yahweh, the God of my lord the king, say so.
verses.explainer: Benaiah enthusiastically confirms the order: 'Amen! May Yahweh confirm it.' His military backing ensures the decree has enforcement power.

annotations.word: Amen. May Yahweh, the God of my lord the king, say so

verses.text: As Yahweh has been with my lord the king, even so may he be with Solomon, and make his throne greater than the throne of my lord king David.”
verses.explainer: Benaiah prays that God will make Solomon's throne even greater than David's -- a bold prayer that will be abundantly fulfilled.

annotations.word: make his throne greater than the throne of my lord king David

verses.text: So Zadok the priest, Nathan the prophet, Benaiah the son of Jehoiada, and the Cherethites and the Pelethites went down and had Solomon ride on king David’s mule, and brought him to Gihon.
verses.explainer: Solomon rides David's mule to Gihon, accompanied by Zadok, Nathan, Benaiah, and the Cherethites and Pelethites -- the royal bodyguard that ensures no opposition.

annotations.word: the Cherethites and the Pelethites

verses.text: Zadok the priest took the horn of oil from the Tent, and anointed Solomon. They blew the trumpet; and all the people said, “Long live king Solomon!”
verses.explainer: Zadok takes the horn of oil from the tent (the tabernacle) and anoints Solomon. The trumpet sounds and the people shout 'Long live king Solomon!'

crossReferences.connection: Samuel anointed David from a horn of oil in the same manner -- Solomon's anointing follows the precedent of his father's

verses.text: All the people came up after him, and the people piped with pipes, and rejoiced with great joy, so that the earth shook with their sound.
verses.explainer: The celebration is so loud that the earth splits with the sound. The entire city erupts in joy for Solomon's coronation.

verses.text: Adonijah and all the guests who were with him heard it as they had finished eating. When Joab heard the sound of the trumpet, he said, “Why is this noise of the city being in an uproar?”
verses.explainer: Adonijah and his guests hear the noise as they finish their feast. Joab hears the trumpet and asks: what does the noise from the city mean?

annotations.word: Joab heard the sound of the trumpet

verses.text: While he yet spoke, behold, Jonathan the son of Abiathar the priest came; and Adonijah said, “Come in; for you are a worthy man, and bring good news.”
verses.explainer: Jonathan son of Abiathar arrives with news. Adonijah assumes he brings good news because he is a 'worthy man' -- but the news is devastating.

annotations.word: Jonathan the son of Abiathar the priest

verses.text: Jonathan answered Adonijah, “Most certainly our lord king David has made Solomon king.
verses.explainer: Jonathan reports: David has made Solomon king. The detailed account of the anointing -- the mule, Gihon, Zadok, Nathan -- confirms it was David's authorized act.

verses.text: The king has sent with him Zadok the priest, Nathan the prophet, Benaiah the son of Jehoiada, and the Cherethites and the Pelethites; and they have caused him to ride on the king’s mule.
verses.explainer: Jonathan lists the officials who participated: Zadok, Nathan, Benaiah, and the bodyguard. The institutional backing makes Adonijah's claim obsolete.

verses.text: Zadok the priest and Nathan the prophet have anointed him king in Gihon. They have come up from there rejoicing, so that the city rang again. This is the noise that you have heard.
verses.explainer: Solomon is already anointed and seated on the throne. The city rejoices. Jonathan's report makes clear: this is a done deal.

annotations.word: Zadok the priest and Nathan the prophet have anointed him king in Gihon

verses.text: Also, Solomon sits on the throne of the kingdom.
verses.explainer: Solomon already sits on the royal throne. The transfer of power is complete before Adonijah even learns about it.

annotations.word: Solomon sits on the throne of the kingdom

verses.text: Moreover the king’s servants came to bless our lord king David, saying, ‘May your God make the name of Solomon better than your name, and make his throne greater than your throne;’ and the king bowed himself on the bed.
verses.explainer: David's servants have come to bless David, and David worshiped God from his bed. The dying king's last significant act is worship after securing the succession.

annotations.word: the king bowed himself on the bed

verses.text: Also thus said the king, ‘Blessed be Yahweh, the God of Israel, who has given one to sit on my throne today, my eyes even seeing it.’”
verses.explainer: David blesses Yahweh for letting him see Solomon seated on the throne with his own eyes. He dies at peace, knowing the covenant continues.

annotations.word: who has given one to sit on my throne today, my eyes even seeing it

verses.text: All the guests of Adonijah were afraid, and rose up, and each man went his way.
verses.explainer: Adonijah's supporters panic and scatter. The feast dissolves as every guest races to distance himself from the failed coup.

verses.text: Adonijah was afraid because of Solomon; and he arose, and went, and hung onto the horns of the altar.
verses.explainer: Adonijah himself flees to the altar and grabs its horns, claiming sanctuary. The horns of the altar were a recognized place of asylum.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: The horns of the altar provided temporary sanctuary -- the person could not be dragged away without profaning the altar. However, this protection was not absolute for deliberate murderers (Exodus 21:14). Adonijah's claim depends on Solomon's willingness to show mercy.

verses.text: Solomon was told, “Behold, Adonijah fears king Solomon; for, behold, he is hanging onto the horns of the altar, saying, ‘Let king Solomon swear to me first that he will not kill his servant with the sword.’”
verses.explainer: Adonijah begs Solomon to swear not to kill him. He stays at the altar until Solomon guarantees his safety.

annotations.word: Let king Solomon swear to me first that he will not kill his servant

verses.text: Solomon said, “If he shows himself a worthy man, not a hair of his shall fall to the earth; but if wickedness is found in him, he shall die.”
verses.explainer: Solomon's response is measured: if Adonijah shows himself worthy, he will live; if wickedness is found in him, he will die. Conditional mercy.

verses.text: So king Solomon sent, and they brought him down from the altar. He came and bowed down to king Solomon; and Solomon said to him, “Go to your house.”
verses.explainer: Solomon sends for Adonijah, who prostrates himself before the new king. Solomon dismisses him to his house -- alive but under watch.

annotations.word: Go to your house

explainer.summary: David gives Solomon final instructions: follow God's law, and deal with Joab and Shimei. David dies. Adonijah makes a fatal request for Abishag, which Solomon interprets as a claim to the throne. Solomon executes Adonijah, banishes Abiathar, and kills Joab and Shimei, consolidating his power completely.
themes: The transfer of power through death
Unfinished justice from the previous reign
explainer.contextInBook: Solomon's early reign involves the systematic elimination of threats David left unresolved. The executions of Adonijah, Joab, and Shimei are presented as both political necessity and the completion of justice David could not or would not execute himself.

wordStudies.transliteration: Be strong, show yourself a man
wordStudies.translatedAs: David's charge to Solomon echoes God's charge to J
wordStudies.meaning: David's charge to Solomon echoes God's charge to Joshua (Joshua 1:6-7). 'Be a man' means not physical toughness but moral and spiritual courage to keep God's law regardless of political pressure.

wordStudies.translatedAs: David tells Solomon to deal with Joab and Shimei '
wordStudies.meaning: David tells Solomon to deal with Joab and Shimei 'according to your wisdom' -- finding the right occasion and method. Political wisdom is the ability to execute justice at the right time and in the right way.

verses.text: Now the days of David came near that he should die; and he commanded Solomon his son, saying,
verses.explainer: David's death approaches. He gives Solomon his last instructions -- a combination of spiritual charge and political counsel.

verses.text: “I am going the way of all the earth. You be strong therefore, and show yourself a man;
verses.explainer: David charges Solomon to be strong and show himself a man -- language that echoes God's charge to Joshua when taking over from Moses.

crossReferences.connection: God's charge to Joshua uses nearly identical language -- be strong and courageous, keep the law. Solomon receives the same commission for the same reason

verses.text: and keep the instruction of Yahweh your God, to walk in his ways, to keep his statutes, his commandments, his ordinances, and his testimonies, according to that which is written in the law of Moses, that you may prosper in all that you do, and wherever you turn yourself.
verses.explainer: David's primary instruction: keep God's law as written in the law of Moses. If Solomon obeys, God will fulfill the Davidic covenant promise.

annotations.word: that you may prosper in all that you do

crossReferences.connection: The same promise of prosperity through obedience that Moses gave Israel -- now applied specifically to the Davidic dynasty

verses.text: Then Yahweh may establish his word which he spoke concerning me, saying, ‘If your children are careful of their way, to walk before me in truth with all their heart and with all their soul, there shall not fail you,’ he said, ‘a man on the throne of Israel.’
verses.explainer: David connects personal obedience to dynastic survival: if Solomon and his descendants walk faithfully, God will keep His promise of an eternal throne.

crossReferences.connection: The Davidic covenant promising an eternal dynasty -- conditional in its immediate application though unconditional in its ultimate fulfillment through the Messiah

verses.text: “Moreover you know also what Joab the son of Zeruiah did to me, even what he did to the two captains of the armies of Israel, to Abner the son of Ner, and to Amasa the son of Jether, whom he killed, and shed the blood of war in peace, and put the blood of war on his sash that was around his waist, and in his shoes that were on his feet.
verses.explainer: David turns to unfinished business: Joab's murders of Abner and Amasa. David charges Solomon to deal with Joab's bloodguilt.

annotations.word: what Joab the son of Zeruiah did to me
shed the blood of war in peace

verses.text: Do therefore according to your wisdom, and don’t let his gray head go down to Sheol in peace.
verses.explainer: David tells Solomon to deal with Joab 'according to your wisdom' -- find the right time and method. Do not let his gray head go down to Sheol in peace.

verses.text: But show kindness to the sons of Barzillai the Gileadite, and let them be among those who eat at your table; for so they came to me when I fled from Absalom your brother.
verses.explainer: David instructs Solomon to show kindness to the sons of Barzillai, who supported David during Absalom's rebellion. Loyalty must be rewarded.

annotations.word: show kindness to the sons of Barzillai the Gileadite

verses.text: “Behold, there is with you Shimei the son of Gera, the Benjamite, of Bahurim, who cursed me with a grievous curse in the day when I went to Mahanaim; but he came down to meet me at the Jordan, and I swore to him by Yahweh, saying, ‘I will not put you to death with the sword.’
verses.explainer: David addresses Shimei, who cursed him during the flight from Absalom. David swore not to kill Shimei himself, but Solomon is not bound by that oath.

annotations.word: Shimei the son of Gera
I swore to him by Yahweh

verses.text: Now therefore don’t hold him guiltless, for you are a wise man; and you will know what you ought to do to him, and you shall bring his gray head down to Sheol with blood.”
verses.explainer: David tells Solomon not to hold Shimei guiltless. Solomon's wisdom will find the right way to bring his gray head down to Sheol with blood.

verses.text: David slept with his fathers, and was buried in David’s city.
verses.explainer: David dies and is buried in the City of David. His forty-year reign ends with the succession securely in Solomon's hands.

annotations.word: David slept with his fathers

verses.text: The days that David reigned over Israel were forty years; he reigned seven years in Hebron, and he reigned thirty-three years in Jerusalem.
verses.explainer: David reigned forty years total: seven in Hebron over Judah, thirty-three in Jerusalem over all Israel. A complete reign spanning the entire monarchy.

annotations.word: forty years

verses.text: Solomon sat on David his father’s throne; and his kingdom was firmly established.
verses.explainer: Solomon sits on David's throne and his kingdom is firmly established. The transition is complete -- the most dangerous moment in any monarchy has been navigated successfully.

annotations.word: his kingdom was firmly established

verses.text:
Then Adonijah the son of Haggith came to Bathsheba the mother of Solomon. She said, “Do you come peaceably?”
He said, “Peaceably.
verses.explainer: Adonijah approaches Bathsheba with a request. His opening -- 'Do you come in peace?' -- already suggests Bathsheba is wary of him.

annotations.word: Adonijah the son of Haggith came to Bathsheba

verses.text:
He said moreover, I have something to tell you.”
She said, “Say on.”
verses.explainer: Adonijah asks to speak. Bathsheba agrees to listen. The scene is tense with unspoken political danger.

verses.text: He said, “You know that the kingdom was mine, and that all Israel set their faces on me, that I should reign. However the kingdom is turned around, and has become my brother’s; for it was his from Yahweh.
verses.explainer: Adonijah claims the kingdom was his by right and all Israel expected him to reign. He acknowledges that God gave the kingdom to Solomon instead.

annotations.word: the kingdom was mine, and that all Israel set their faces on me

verses.text:
Now I ask one petition of you. Don’t deny me.”
She said to him, “Say on.”
verses.explainer: Adonijah asks Bathsheba for one thing, begging her not to refuse. The request seems modest but is politically explosive.

verses.text: He said, “Please speak to Solomon the king (for he will not tell you ‘no’), that he give me Abishag the Shunammite as wife.”
verses.explainer: Adonijah asks Bathsheba to request Abishag the Shunammite as his wife. Taking the former king's attendant was a recognized claim to the throne.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: In the ancient Near East, taking a king's harem was a claim to his throne. Absalom took David's concubines to claim kingship. Adonijah's request for Abishag, whether naive or calculated, amounts to the same thing -- a renewed claim to the throne that Solomon will not tolerate.

verses.text: Bathsheba said, “Alright. I will speak for you to the king.”
verses.explainer: Bathsheba agrees to speak to Solomon on Adonijah's behalf. Whether she understands the political implications or is testing Adonijah is debatable.

annotations.word: I will speak for you to the king

verses.text: Bathsheba therefore went to king Solomon, to speak to him for Adonijah. The king rose up to meet her, and bowed himself to her, and sat down on his throne, and caused a throne to be set for the king’s mother; and she sat on his right hand.
verses.explainer: Bathsheba comes to Solomon, who rises to meet her, bows, and seats her on a throne at his right hand. The queen mother receives the highest honor.

verses.text:
Then she said, “I ask one small petition of you; don’t deny me.”
The king said to her, “Ask on, my mother; for I will not deny you.”
verses.explainer: Bathsheba presents Adonijah's request as a 'small petition.' Solomon promises not to refuse her before hearing the request.

annotations.word: I ask one small petition of you

verses.text: She said, “Let Abishag the Shunammite be given to Adonijah your brother as wife.”
verses.explainer: Bathsheba asks Solomon to give Abishag to Adonijah as wife. The request is straightforward but its implications are explosive.

annotations.word: Let Abishag the Shunammite be given to Adonijah your brother as wife

verses.text: King Solomon answered his mother, “Why do you ask Abishag the Shunammite for Adonijah? Ask for him the kingdom also; for he is my elder brother; even for him, and for Abiathar the priest, and for Joab the son of Zeruiah.”
verses.explainer: Solomon explodes: why ask for Abishag? Why not ask for the kingdom too! He sees through the request to its political meaning and includes Adonijah's allies in the threat assessment.

annotations.word: for he is my elder brother

verses.text: Then king Solomon swore by Yahweh, saying, “God do so to me, and more also, if Adonijah has not spoken this word against his own life.
verses.explainer: Solomon swears by God that Adonijah has sealed his own death sentence. The request for Abishag has spoken against his own life.

annotations.word: God do so to me, and more also, if Adonijah has not spoken this word against his own life

verses.text: Now therefore as Yahweh lives, who has established me, and set me on my father David’s throne, and who has made me a house as he promised, surely Adonijah shall be put to death today.”
verses.explainer: Solomon executes the sentence immediately through Benaiah. Adonijah's attempt to reclaim the throne through Abishag costs him his life.

annotations.word: Adonijah shall be put to death today

verses.text: King Solomon sent Benaiah the son of Jehoiada; and he fell on him, so that he died.
verses.explainer: Benaiah executes Adonijah. The first threat to Solomon's throne is permanently eliminated.

verses.text: To Abiathar the priest the king said, “Go to Anathoth, to your own fields; for you are worthy of death. But I will not at this time put you to death, because you bore the Lord Yahweh’s ark before David my father, and because you were afflicted in all in which my father was afflicted.”
verses.explainer: Solomon banishes Abiathar from the priesthood, sending him to his fields at Anathoth. He spares his life because he carried the ark and shared David's hardships.

annotations.word: Go to Anathoth, to your own fields

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: Abiathar's removal fulfills the prophecy against Eli's house from 1 Samuel 2:30-36. The priestly line shifts from Abiathar (descended from Eli through Ahimelech) to Zadok, whose lineage would dominate until the destruction of the temple.

crossReferences.connection: The prophecy against Eli's priestly house is fulfilled when Abiathar is deposed in favor of Zadok

verses.text: So Solomon thrust Abiathar out from being priest to Yahweh, that he might fulfill Yahweh’s word, which he spoke concerning the house of Eli in Shiloh.
verses.explainer: The narrator notes this fulfills God's word against the house of Eli spoken at Shiloh. The old priestly line ends; Zadok's begins.

verses.text: This news came to Joab; for Joab had followed Adonijah, although he didn’t follow Absalom. Joab fled to Yahweh’s Tent, and held onto the horns of the altar.
verses.explainer: Joab hears the news and flees to the altar, grasping its horns as Adonijah did. He has sided with Adonijah and knows he is next.

verses.text: King Solomon was told, “Joab has fled to Yahweh’s Tent, and behold, he is by the altar.” Then Solomon sent Benaiah the son of Jehoiada, saying, “Go, fall on him.”
verses.explainer: Solomon sends Benaiah to execute Joab at the altar. The sacred space will not protect a deliberate murderer (Exodus 21:14).

annotations.word: Go, fall on him

crossReferences.connection: The law specifying that deliberate murderers can be taken from the altar for execution -- the sanctuary does not protect premeditated killers

verses.text:
Benaiah came to Yahweh’s Tent, and said to him, “Thus says the king, ‘Come out!’”
He said, “No; but I will die here.”
Benaiah brought the king word again, saying, “Thus said Joab, and thus he answered me.”
verses.explainer: Benaiah tells Joab to come away from the altar. Joab refuses: 'No, I will die here.' Benaiah reports back to Solomon for instructions.

annotations.word: I will die here

verses.text: The king said to him, “Do as he has said, and fall on him, and bury him; that you may take away the blood, which Joab shed without cause, from me and from my father’s house.
verses.explainer: Solomon orders Benaiah to strike Joab at the altar and bury him, removing the bloodguilt of Abner and Amasa from David's house.

verses.text: Yahweh will return his blood on his own head, because he fell on two men more righteous and better than he, and killed them with the sword, and my father David didn’t know it: Abner the son of Ner, captain of the army of Israel, and Amasa the son of Jether, captain of the army of Judah.
verses.explainer: Solomon declares that Joab's blood returns on his own head because he murdered two men more righteous than himself without David's knowledge.

annotations.word: two men more righteous and better than he

verses.text: So their blood will return on the head of Joab, and on the head of his offspring forever. But for David, for his offspring, for his house, and for his throne, there will be peace forever from Yahweh.”
verses.explainer: The bloodguilt falls on Joab's descendants, while David's house and throne receive peace from God forever. Justice restores the dynasty's moral standing.

verses.text: Then Benaiah the son of Jehoiada went up and fell on him, and killed him; and he was buried in his own house in the wilderness.
verses.explainer: Benaiah executes Joab at the altar. The general who served David for forty years dies at Solomon's command.

verses.text: The king put Benaiah the son of Jehoiada in his place over the army; and the king put Zadok the priest in the place of Abiathar.
verses.explainer: Solomon appoints Benaiah as army commander and Zadok as sole priest, replacing Joab and Abiathar. The power structure is completely reorganized.

verses.text: The king sent and called for Shimei, and said to him, “Build yourself a house in Jerusalem, and live there, and don’t go anywhere else.
verses.explainer: Solomon summons Shimei and gives him a conditional pardon: live in Jerusalem and never leave. If he crosses the Kidron, he dies.

verses.text: For on the day you go out and pass over the brook Kidron, know for certain that you will surely die. Your blood will be on your own head.”
verses.explainer: The condition is explicit: if Shimei crosses the Kidron brook, he will be executed. Shimei agrees to the terms.

annotations.word: the day you go out and pass over the brook Kidron

verses.text: Shimei said to the king, “What you say is good. As my lord the king has said, so will your servant do.” Shimei lived in Jerusalem many days.
verses.explainer: Shimei agrees and lives in Jerusalem for three years under the restriction. The arrangement works until external circumstances create temptation.

annotations.word: Shimei lived in Jerusalem many days

verses.text: At the end of three years, two of Shimei’s slaves ran away to Achish, son of Maacah, king of Gath. They told Shimei, saying, “Behold, your slaves are in Gath.”
verses.explainer: Two of Shimei's servants run away to the Philistine king of Gath. The temptation to retrieve them draws Shimei out of Jerusalem.
