verses.explainer: Shimei saddles his donkey and goes to Gath to retrieve his servants. He violates the explicit terms of his pardon.

verses.text: Solomon was told that Shimei had gone from Jerusalem to Gath, and had come again.
verses.explainer: Solomon is told of Shimei's trip. The intelligence network David built continues to function under Solomon.

annotations.word: Solomon was told that Shimei had gone from Jerusalem to Gath

verses.text: The king sent and called for Shimei, and said to him, “Didn’t I adjure you by Yahweh, and warn you, saying, ‘Know for certain, that on the day you go out, and walk anywhere else, you shall surely die?’ You said to me, ‘The saying that I have heard is good.’
verses.explainer: Solomon summons Shimei and reminds him of the oath and the explicit warning. Shimei acknowledged the terms and swore to comply.

verses.text: Why then have you not kept the oath of Yahweh, and the commandment that I have instructed you with?”
verses.explainer: Solomon asks why Shimei broke his oath -- a rhetorical question since the answer is obvious: Shimei prioritized property over his own life.

verses.text: The king said moreover to Shimei, “You know in your heart all the wickedness that you did to David my father. Therefore Yahweh will return your wickedness on your own head.
verses.explainer: Solomon declares that Shimei knows in his heart all the evil he did to David. Now Yahweh returns that evil on his own head.

verses.text: But king Solomon will be blessed, and David’s throne will be established before Yahweh forever.”
verses.explainer: Solomon blesses himself and declares that David's throne will be established before Yahweh forever. Justice secures the dynasty's future.

verses.text: So the king commanded Benaiah the son of Jehoiada; and he went out, and fell on him, so that he died. The kingdom was established in the hand of Solomon.
verses.explainer: Benaiah executes Shimei. The chapter concludes: 'So the kingdom was established in the hand of Solomon.' All threats have been eliminated.

explainer.summary: Solomon marries Pharaoh's daughter and sacrifices at the high place of Gibeon. God appears to Solomon in a dream and offers him anything. Solomon asks for wisdom to govern, which pleases God, who grants him wisdom, wealth, and honor. Solomon demonstrates his wisdom by judging the case of two women claiming the same baby.
themes: The beginning of Solomon's golden age
explainer.contextInBook: This chapter establishes Solomon's defining characteristic: divine wisdom. His humble request for an 'understanding heart' contrasts with what he could have asked for (long life, wealth, victory). The famous judgment between the two mothers demonstrates wisdom in action and establishes Solomon's reputation.

wordStudies.translatedAs: Literally a 'hearing heart' or 'listening heart
wordStudies.meaning: Literally a 'hearing heart' or 'listening heart.' Solomon asks not for intellectual knowledge but for the capacity to listen attentively, discern truth, and judge with insight. Wisdom begins with receptive listening.

wordStudies.translatedAs: To distinguish, perceive, or understand -- the abi
wordStudies.meaning: To distinguish, perceive, or understand -- the ability to see the difference between good and evil, truth and deception. Discernment is wisdom applied to specific situations.

verses.text: Solomon made an alliance with Pharaoh king of Egypt, and took Pharaoh’s daughter, and brought her into David’s city, until he had finished building his own house, Yahweh’s house, and the wall around Jerusalem.
verses.explainer: Solomon makes an alliance with Egypt by marrying Pharaoh's daughter. This is his first foreign marriage and a significant diplomatic achievement.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: Egyptian pharaohs almost never gave their daughters in marriage to foreign kings. That Pharaoh agreed to this marriage shows the extraordinary respect Israel commanded under Solomon. The marriage, however, introduces the pattern of foreign alliances that will lead to Solomon's spiritual downfall.

verses.text: However the people sacrificed in the high places, because there was not yet a house built for Yahweh’s name.
verses.explainer: The people still sacrifice at high places because the temple has not yet been built. The narrator explains but does not excuse this irregular worship.

annotations.word: the people sacrificed in the high places

verses.text: Solomon loved Yahweh, walking in the statutes of David his father; except that he sacrificed and burned incense in the high places.
verses.explainer: Solomon loves Yahweh and follows David's statutes, but he also sacrifices at high places. The narrator introduces the tension that will define Solomon's story.

annotations.word: Solomon loved Yahweh

verses.text: The king went to Gibeon to sacrifice there; for that was the great high place. Solomon offered a thousand burnt offerings on that altar.
verses.explainer: Solomon goes to Gibeon, the most important high place, and offers a thousand burnt offerings. Gibeon housed the tabernacle at this time.

annotations.word: a thousand burnt offerings

crossReferences.connection: The parallel explains that the tabernacle of Moses was at Gibeon, making it the legitimate worship site before the temple

verses.text: In Gibeon, Yahweh appeared to Solomon in a dream by night; and God said, “Ask for what I should give you.”
verses.explainer: God appears to Solomon in a dream at Gibeon: 'Ask what I shall give you.' The offer is unlimited -- God invites Solomon to name his desire.

thenVsNow.then: What we would ask for if given an unlimited choice reveals our deepest values. Solomon's request for wisdom rather than wealth or power is why God gives him all three.
thenVsNow.now: What we would ask for if given an unlimited choice reveals our deepest values. Solomon's request for wisdom rather than wealth or power is why God gives him all three.

verses.text: Solomon said, “You have shown to your servant David my father great loving kindness, because he walked before you in truth, in righteousness, and in uprightness of heart with you. You have kept for him this great loving kindness, that you have given him a son to sit on his throne, as it is today.
verses.explainer: Solomon recounts God's kindness to David and acknowledges that his own succession is a gift of God's faithfulness to his father's legacy.

annotations.word: You have shown to your servant David my father great loving kindness

verses.text: Now, Yahweh my God, you have made your servant king instead of David my father. I am just a little child. I don’t know how to go out or come in.
verses.explainer: Solomon confesses his inadequacy: 'I am but a little child; I don't know how to go out or come in.' His humility is the foundation of his wisdom request.

verses.text: Your servant is among your people which you have chosen, a great people, that can’t be numbered or counted for multitude.
verses.explainer: Solomon describes the enormity of the task: ruling a people so vast they cannot be numbered. The scale demands something beyond human ability.

verses.text: Give your servant therefore an understanding heart to judge your people, that I may discern between good and evil; for who is able to judge this great people of yours?”
verses.explainer: Solomon asks for an 'understanding heart' -- literally a 'listening heart' -- to judge the people and discern between good and evil. This is the famous wisdom request.

annotations.word: Give your servant therefore an understanding heart to judge your people
discern between good and evil

crossReferences.connection: James tells believers to ask God for wisdom, and He will give it generously -- the same principle Solomon demonstrates

verses.text: This request pleased the Lord, that Solomon had asked this thing.
verses.explainer: Solomon's request pleases God. The fact that it pleased God implies other requests would not have -- the test reveals Solomon's character.

verses.text: God said to him, “Because you have asked this thing, and have not asked for yourself long life, nor have you asked for riches for yourself, nor have you asked for the life of your enemies, but have asked for yourself understanding to discern justice;
verses.explainer: God notes what Solomon did not ask for: long life, wealth, or the death of his enemies. These are what most kings would have requested.

verses.text: behold, I have done according to your word. Behold, I have given you a wise and understanding heart; so that there has been no one like you before you, and after you none will arise like you.
verses.explainer: God grants Solomon a wise and understanding heart unmatched before or after him. The gift exceeds the request -- divine generosity responds to human humility.

annotations.word: there has been no one like you before you, and after you none will arise like you

verses.text: I have also given you that which you have not asked, both riches and honor, so that there will not be any among the kings like you for all your days.
verses.explainer: God also gives what Solomon did not ask for: riches and honor surpassing all contemporary kings. The unasked-for gifts outstrip what most kings desire most.

annotations.word: I have also given you that which you have not asked

crossReferences.connection: Jesus teaches the same principle: seek first the kingdom and righteousness, and all these things will be added to you

verses.text: If you will walk in my ways, to keep my statutes and my commandments, as your father David walked, then I will lengthen your days.”
verses.explainer: God adds a conditional promise of long life: if Solomon walks in God's ways as David did, his days will be lengthened. The condition is significant.

verses.text: Solomon awoke; and behold, it was a dream. Then he came to Jerusalem, and stood before the ark of Yahweh’s covenant, and offered up burnt offerings, offered peace offerings, and made a feast for all his servants.
verses.explainer: Solomon wakes and realizes it was a dream. He returns to Jerusalem, stands before the ark, and offers sacrifices. The dream has changed him.

annotations.word: behold, it was a dream

verses.text: Then two women who were prostitutes came to the king, and stood before him.
verses.explainer: Two prostitutes come before Solomon with a dispute over a living baby. This case will demonstrate the wisdom God just granted.

annotations.word: two women who were prostitutes

verses.text: The one woman said, “Oh, my lord, I and this woman dwell in one house. I delivered a child with her in the house.
verses.explainer: The first woman explains they live together and both recently gave birth. No one else was present -- there are no witnesses to establish the truth.

verses.text: The third day after I delivered, this woman delivered also. We were together. There was no stranger with us in the house, just us two in the house.
verses.explainer: The second woman gave birth three days later. Both have newborns, living in the same house, with no third-party witnesses.

verses.text: This woman’s child died in the night, because she lay on it.
verses.explainer: The first woman accuses the second of accidentally smothering her own baby by rolling over on it during the night.

verses.text: She arose at midnight, and took my son from beside me, while your servant slept, and laid it in her bosom, and laid her dead child in my bosom.
verses.explainer: The first woman claims the other switched the babies at midnight, taking the living child and leaving the dead one.

verses.text: When I rose in the morning to nurse my child, behold, it was dead; but when I had looked at it in the morning, behold, it was not my son, whom I bore.”
verses.explainer: The first woman says she realized the switch when she tried to nurse the dead baby and saw it was not her son. The identification happened in morning light.

verses.text:
The other woman said, “No; but the living one is my son, and the dead one is your son.”
The first one said, “No; but the dead one is your son, and the living one is my son.” Thus they spoke before the king.
verses.explainer: The second woman contradicts: the living child is mine and the dead one is yours. The dispute is a classic 'he said, she said' with no external evidence.

verses.text: Then the king said, “One says, ‘This is my son who lives, and your son is the dead;’ and the other says, ‘No; but your son is the dead one, and my son is the living one.’”
verses.explainer: Solomon restates the impasse: both women claim the living child. No conventional legal method can determine the truth.

verses.text: The king said, “Get me a sword.” So they brought a sword before the king.
verses.explainer: Solomon calls for a sword. The courtroom must have gone silent with shock and fear at the king's command.

verses.text: The king said, “Divide the living child in two, and give half to the one, and half to the other.”
verses.explainer: Solomon orders the living baby cut in two, with half given to each woman. The command is designed to provoke the real mother's instinctive response.

annotations.word: Divide the living child in two

verses.text:
Then the woman whose the living child was spoke to the king, for her heart yearned over her son, and she said, “Oh, my lord, give her the living child, and in no way kill him!”
But the other said, “He shall be neither mine nor yours. Divide him.”
verses.explainer: The real mother's compassion overwhelms her: 'Give her the living child! Don't kill it!' The false mother says: 'Let it be neither mine nor yours -- divide it.'

annotations.word: her heart yearned over her son

thenVsNow.then: Solomon's judgment works because genuine love would rather lose than destroy, while counterfeit love would rather destroy than lose.
thenVsNow.now: Solomon's judgment works because genuine love would rather lose than destroy, while counterfeit love would rather destroy than lose.

verses.text: Then the king answered, “Give her the living child, and definitely do not kill him. She is his mother.”
verses.explainer: Solomon declares: give the baby to the first woman. She is the real mother -- the one whose love for the child overrode her desire to win the case.

verses.text: All Israel heard of the judgment which the king had judged; and they feared the king; for they saw that the wisdom of God was in him, to do justice.
verses.explainer: All Israel hears of the judgment and stands in awe of Solomon's wisdom. They recognize that divine wisdom, not just human intelligence, is at work in their king.

annotations.word: the wisdom of God was in him, to do justice

explainer.summary: Solomon's administrative structure is laid out: officials, twelve district governors who supply the royal household, and the vast extent of his kingdom. Israel is prosperous and at peace, and Solomon's wisdom surpasses all contemporaries. He composes thousands of proverbs and songs.
themes: Prosperity as the fruit of wisdom
explainer.contextInBook: This chapter demonstrates the practical fruits of Solomon's wisdom: an efficient government, a prosperous nation, and international prestige. It presents the idealized picture of what Israel looks like when ruled by a wise king who follows God -- the fulfillment of the promises in chapter 3.

wordStudies.translatedAs: The multitude of Israel under Solomon fulfills the
wordStudies.meaning: The multitude of Israel under Solomon fulfills the patriarchal promise that Abraham's descendants would be like sand on the seashore. Population growth is presented as the visible blessing of wise, godly governance.

wordStudies.translatedAs: Practical skill for living well, encompassing gove
wordStudies.meaning: Practical skill for living well, encompassing governance, natural science, literature, and ethical discernment. Solomon's wisdom is not abstract philosophy but applied knowledge across every domain of life.

verses.text: King Solomon was king over all Israel.
verses.explainer: Solomon rules over all Israel. This summary statement introduces the detailed administrative structure that follows.

annotations.word: Solomon was king over all Israel

verses.text: These were the princes whom he had: Azariah the son of Zadok, the priest;
verses.explainer: The list of Solomon's chief officials begins. These are the cabinet-level administrators who run the kingdom.

verses.text: Elihoreph and Ahijah, the sons of Shisha, scribes; Jehoshaphat the son of Ahilud, the recorder;
verses.explainer: Elihoreph and Ahijah are scribes (secretaries of state); Jehoshaphat is the recorder (royal historian and communications director).

annotations.word: scribes

verses.text: Benaiah the son of Jehoiada was over the army; Zadok and Abiathar were priests;
verses.explainer: Benaiah commands the army; Zadok and Abiathar are named as priests (though Abiathar has been removed -- this may reflect an earlier source or a titular honor).

annotations.word: Benaiah the son of Jehoiada was over the army

verses.text: Azariah the son of Nathan was over the officers; Zabud the son of Nathan was chief minister, the king’s friend;
verses.explainer: Azariah oversees the district governors; Zabud is the king's personal advisor and priest -- a 'friend of the king,' the most intimate counselor position.

verses.text: Ahishar was over the household; and Adoniram the son of Abda was over the men subject to forced labor.
verses.explainer: Ahishar runs the royal household; Adoniram oversees the forced labor. The mention of forced labor foreshadows the grievance that will split the kingdom.

crossReferences.connection: Adoniram is stoned by the northern tribes when Rehoboam sends him to collect labor -- the administrator becomes the symbol of oppression

verses.text: Solomon had twelve officers over all Israel, who provided food for the king and his household. Each man had to make provision for a month in the year.
verses.explainer: Solomon establishes twelve district governors over all Israel, each responsible for provisioning the royal household for one month per year.

annotations.word: twelve officers over all Israel

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: The twelve districts did not correspond exactly to the twelve tribal territories, suggesting Solomon deliberately reorganized the administrative map to break down tribal identities and centralize power. This was efficient but resented.

verses.text: These are their names: Ben Hur, in the hill country of Ephraim;
verses.explainer: The first district governor is listed. The detailed list that follows demonstrates Solomon's sophisticated administrative system.

verses.text: Ben Deker, in Makaz, in Shaalbim, Beth Shemesh, and Elon Beth Hanan;
verses.explainer: Ben-deker governs the second district in the western lowlands -- Makaz, Shaalbim, Beth-shemesh, and Elon-beth-hanan.

verses.text: Ben Hesed, in Arubboth (Socoh and all the land of Hepher belonged to him);
verses.explainer: Ben-hesed administers the coastal district including Arubboth, Socoh, and all of Hepher.

verses.text: Ben Abinadab, in all the height of Dor (he had Taphath, Solomon’s daughter, as wife);
verses.explainer: Ben-abinadab governs the Dor region on the coast. He is married to Solomon's daughter Taphath, connecting the administration to the royal family.

verses.text: Baana the son of Ahilud, in Taanach and Megiddo, and all Beth Shean which is beside Zarethan, beneath Jezreel, from Beth Shean to Abel Meholah, as far as beyond Jokmeam;
verses.explainer: Baana son of Ahilud administers the Jezreel Valley region -- Taanach, Megiddo, and Beth-shean. One of the most strategically important districts.

annotations.word: Taanach and Megiddo

verses.text: Ben Geber, in Ramoth Gilead (the towns of Jair the son of Manasseh, which are in Gilead, belonged to him; and the region of Argob, which is in Bashan, sixty great cities with walls and bronze bars, belonged to him);
verses.explainer: Ben-geber governs Ramoth-gilead in the Transjordan, including the towns of Jair and the region of Argob with its sixty walled cities.

annotations.word: sixty great cities with walls and bronze bars

verses.text: Ahinadab the son of Iddo, in Mahanaim;
verses.explainer: Ahinadab son of Iddo administers Mahanaim, David's refuge during Absalom's rebellion.

annotations.word: Mahanaim

verses.text: Ahimaaz, in Naphtali (he also took Basemath the daughter of Solomon as wife);
verses.explainer: Ahimaaz governs the Naphtali district. He has married Solomon's daughter Basemath -- another connection of administration to the royal family.

verses.text: Baana the son of Hushai, in Asher and Bealoth;
verses.explainer: Baana son of Hushai administers Asher and Bealoth on the northern coast. Hushai may be the same counselor who served David.

annotations.word: Baana the son of Hushai

verses.text: Jehoshaphat the son of Paruah, in Issachar;
verses.explainer: Jehoshaphat son of Paruah governs the Issachar district -- a fertile agricultural region in the eastern Jezreel Valley.

annotations.word: Jehoshaphat the son of Paruah, in Issachar

verses.text: Shimei the son of Ela, in Benjamin;
verses.explainer: Shimei son of Ela administers the Benjamin district. Placing an administrator over Benjamin keeps David's rival tribe under close oversight.

annotations.word: Shimei the son of Ela, in Benjamin

verses.text: Geber the son of Uri, in the land of Gilead, the country of Sihon king of the Amorites and of Og king of Bashan; and he was the only officer who was in the land.
verses.explainer: Geber son of Uri governs the Gilead region east of the Jordan -- the sole governor for a territory that once had two rival kingdoms.

annotations.word: he was the only officer who was in the land

verses.text: Judah and Israel were numerous as the sand which is by the sea in multitude, eating and drinking and making merry.
verses.explainer: Israel is as numerous as sand on the seashore. They eat, drink, and are happy. This is the fulfillment of the patriarchal promise under wise governance.

crossReferences.connection: God's promise that Abraham's descendants would be as the sand of the sea -- fulfilled under Solomon's prosperous reign

verses.text: Solomon ruled over all the kingdoms from the River to the land of the Philistines, and to the border of Egypt. They brought tribute and served Solomon all the days of his life.
verses.explainer: Solomon rules from the Euphrates to the border of Egypt. All the subjected kingdoms pay tribute. This represents the maximum extent of Israelite territory.

annotations.word: from the River to the land of the Philistines, and to the border of Egypt

crossReferences.connection: God's covenant with Abraham promising the land from Egypt to the Euphrates -- completely fulfilled for the first and only time under Solomon

verses.text: Solomon’s provision for one day was thirty cors of fine flour, sixty measures of meal,
verses.explainer: Solomon's daily provision: thirty measures of fine flour, sixty measures of meal -- enough to feed the massive royal household and administration.

verses.text: ten head of fat cattle, twenty head of cattle out of the pastures, and one hundred sheep, besides deer, and gazelles, and roebucks, and fattened fowl.
verses.explainer: Daily meat includes ten fattened cattle, twenty pasture-fed cattle, and a hundred sheep, plus various game. The royal table feeds hundreds daily.

verses.text: For he had dominion over all on this side the River, from Tiphsah even to Gaza, over all the kings on this side the River: and he had peace on all sides around him.
verses.explainer: Solomon rules over everything west of the Euphrates, from Tiphsah to Gaza, and has peace on every side.

annotations.word: he had peace on all sides around him

verses.text: Judah and Israel lived safely, every man under his vine and under his fig tree, from Dan even to Beersheba, all the days of Solomon.
verses.explainer: Every man sits under his own vine and fig tree -- the biblical image of complete peace and prosperity. This is the golden age at its zenith.

annotations.word: every man under his vine and under his fig tree

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: The vine and fig tree image becomes the prophetic ideal of the messianic age (Micah 4:4, Zechariah 3:10). Solomon's reign is the closest Old Testament Israel comes to this ideal, making it the template for future hopes of restoration.

crossReferences.connection: The prophetic vision of the messianic age uses the same image: every man under his vine and fig tree, with no one to make him afraid

thenVsNow.then: The image of every person secure under their own vine and fig tree remains one of the Bible's most powerful pictures of the peace God intends for His people.
thenVsNow.now: The image of every person secure under their own vine and fig tree remains one of the Bible's most powerful pictures of the peace God intends for His people.

verses.text: Solomon had forty thousand stalls of horses for his chariots, and twelve thousand horsemen.
verses.explainer: Solomon has forty thousand stalls for chariot horses and twelve thousand horsemen -- a massive military establishment maintained during peacetime.

annotations.word: forty thousand stalls of horses for his chariots

verses.text: Those officers provided food for king Solomon, and for all who came to king Solomon’s table, every man in his month. They let nothing be lacking.
verses.explainer: The twelve district governors each supply provisions for one month, ensuring the royal household lacks nothing.

verses.text: They also brought Barley and straw for the horses and swift steeds to the place where the officers were, each man according to his duty.
verses.explainer: Barley and straw for the horses are delivered to the appointed places. Even the logistics of animal feed are systematically organized.

verses.text: God gave Solomon abundant wisdom and understanding, and very great understanding, even as the sand that is on the seashore.
verses.explainer: God gives Solomon wisdom, understanding, and largeness of heart like the sand on the seashore. His intellectual capacity is described in cosmic terms.

verses.text: Solomon’s wisdom excelled the wisdom of all the children of the east and all the wisdom of Egypt.
verses.explainer: Solomon's wisdom surpasses all the wise men of the East and of Egypt -- the two great centers of ancient wisdom tradition.

verses.text: For he was wiser than all men; than Ethan the Ezrahite, Heman, Calcol, and Darda, the sons of Mahol: and his fame was in all the nations all around.
verses.explainer: Solomon is wiser than specific legendary sages: Ethan, Heman, Calcol, and Darda. His fame spreads to all surrounding nations.

annotations.word: he was wiser than all men

verses.text: He spoke three thousand proverbs; and his songs numbered one thousand five.
verses.explainer: Solomon composes three thousand proverbs and one thousand five songs. His literary output is vast, establishing him as the patron of Israel's wisdom literature.

verses.text: He spoke of trees, from the cedar that is in Lebanon even to the hyssop that grows out of the wall; he also spoke of animals, of birds, of creeping things, and of fish.
verses.explainer: Solomon's knowledge encompasses botany and zoology: from the cedar of Lebanon to the hyssop on the wall, from beasts to fish.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: Solomon's botanical and zoological knowledge represents the ancient Near Eastern wisdom tradition's interest in classifying and understanding the natural world. This encyclopedic knowledge was considered a mark of divine wisdom.

verses.text: People of all nations came to hear the wisdom of Solomon, sent by all kings of the earth, who had heard of his wisdom.
verses.explainer: People come from all nations to hear Solomon's wisdom, sent by kings who hear of his reputation. Israel becomes a center of international learning.

explainer.summary: Solomon negotiates with Hiram of Tyre for cedar and cypress timber and skilled labor for the temple. In exchange, Solomon provides wheat and oil. He organizes a massive labor force of thirty thousand Israelites to work in Lebanon in rotating shifts.
themes: The cost of building God's house
explainer.contextInBook: David wanted to build the temple but was told he was a man of war (1 Chronicles 22:8). Solomon's peaceful reign creates the conditions for temple construction. The alliance with Tyre provides the materials and expertise Israel lacks.

wordStudies.translatedAs: God has given Solomon 'rest' on every side -- the
wordStudies.meaning: God has given Solomon 'rest' on every side -- the same word for the Sabbath rest. Peace from enemies creates the conditions for building God's house, just as the Sabbath creates space for worship.

wordStudies.translatedAs: Conscripted labor drafted for royal building proje
wordStudies.meaning: Conscripted labor drafted for royal building projects. This levy of thirty thousand men was deeply resented and became one of the primary grievances that split the kingdom after Solomon's death.

verses.text: Hiram king of Tyre sent his servants to Solomon; for he had heard that they had anointed him king in the place of his father, and Hiram had always loved David.
verses.explainer: Hiram king of Tyre sends ambassadors to Solomon upon hearing of his anointing. Hiram had been David's ally and now seeks to continue the relationship.

annotations.word: Hiram king of Tyre

verses.text: Solomon sent to Hiram, saying,
verses.explainer: Solomon sends word to Hiram explaining his plan to build a temple for Yahweh, something David was unable to do because of his wars.

annotations.word: Solomon sent to Hiram

verses.text: “You know that David my father could not build a house for the name of Yahweh his God because of the wars which were around him on every side, until Yahweh put his enemies under the soles of his feet.
verses.explainer: Solomon explains that David could not build the temple because of the wars God put under his feet. The wars were necessary but disqualifying for temple building.

annotations.word: David my father could not build a house

crossReferences.connection: God told David he had shed too much blood to build the temple -- the task would fall to his son of peace, Solomon

verses.text: But now Yahweh my God has given me rest on every side. There is no enemy and no evil occurrence.
verses.explainer: Solomon declares that God has given him rest on every side -- no adversary, no disaster. The peaceful conditions make temple building possible.

annotations.word: Yahweh my God has given me rest on every side

verses.text: Behold, I intend to build a house for the name of Yahweh my God, as Yahweh spoke to David my father, saying, ‘Your son, whom I will set on your throne in your place shall build the house for my name.’
verses.explainer: Solomon announces his intention to build a house for the name of Yahweh, fulfilling God's promise to David that his son would build it.

annotations.word: I intend to build a house for the name of Yahweh

crossReferences.connection: God's promise that David's son would build a house for God's name -- now being fulfilled

verses.text: Now therefore command that cedar trees be cut for me out of Lebanon. My servants will be with your servants; and I will give you wages for your servants according to all that you say. For you know that there is nobody among us who knows how to cut timber like the Sidonians.”
verses.explainer: Solomon requests cedar from Lebanon, offering to send his workers alongside Hiram's. He acknowledges that no Israelites match the Sidonians' skill in cutting timber.

verses.text: When Hiram heard the words of Solomon, he rejoiced greatly, and said, “Blessed is Yahweh today, who has given to David a wise son to rule over this great people.”
verses.explainer: Hiram rejoices at Solomon's request, praising Yahweh for giving David a wise son. A Phoenician king acknowledges Israel's God.

annotations.word: Blessed is Yahweh today, who has given to David a wise son

verses.text: Hiram sent to Solomon, saying, “I have heard the message which you have sent to me. I will do all your desire concerning timber of cedar, and concerning cypress timber.
verses.explainer: Hiram agrees to supply all the cedar and cypress Solomon needs, with the timber floated down the coast as rafts to a port Solomon designates.

verses.text: My servants will bring them down from Lebanon to the sea. I will make them into rafts to go by sea to the place that you specify to me, and will cause them to be broken up there, and you will receive them. You will accomplish my desire, in giving food for my household.”
verses.explainer: Hiram's terms: his servants will bring the timber to the sea and float it to Solomon's designated location. Solomon must provide food for Hiram's household.

verses.text: So Hiram gave Solomon cedar timber and cypress timber according to all his desire.
verses.explainer: Hiram provides all the cedar and cypress Solomon wants. The supply is abundant and continuous throughout the building project.

verses.text: Solomon gave Hiram twenty thousand cors of wheat for food to his household, and twenty cors of pure oil. Solomon gave this to Hiram year by year.
verses.explainer: Solomon pays Hiram annually: twenty thousand measures of wheat and twenty measures of pure oil. The payment feeds Hiram's household and court.

verses.text: Yahweh gave Solomon wisdom, as he promised him. There was peace between Hiram and Solomon, and the two of them made a treaty together.
verses.explainer: God gives Solomon wisdom as promised, and there is peace between Hiram and Solomon. They make a formal treaty.

annotations.word: Yahweh gave Solomon wisdom, as he promised him

verses.text: King Solomon raised a levy out of all Israel; and the levy was thirty thousand men.
verses.explainer: Solomon conscripts thirty thousand men from all Israel for forced labor. This levy will become one of the kingdom's most resented policies.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: Forced labor (mas) was the primary grievance that caused the kingdom to split after Solomon's death (1 Kings 12:4). The same practice that built the temple also sowed the seeds of national division.

verses.text: He sent them to Lebanon, ten thousand a month by courses; for a month they were in Lebanon, and two months at home; and Adoniram was over the men subject to forced labor.
verses.explainer: The workers serve in rotating shifts: ten thousand per month in Lebanon, with two months home. Adoniram oversees the entire labor system.

annotations.word: a month they were in Lebanon, and two months at home

verses.text: Solomon had seventy thousand who bore burdens, and eighty thousand who were stone cutters in the mountains;
verses.explainer: Solomon employs seventy thousand burden-bearers and eighty thousand stonecutters in the hill country. The workforce is enormous by any ancient standard.

annotations.word: seventy thousand who bore burdens

verses.text: besides Solomon’s chief officers who were over the work, three thousand and three hundred, who ruled over the people who labored in the work.
verses.explainer: Three thousand three hundred supervisors oversee the workers. The management structure reflects sophisticated project organization.

verses.text: The king commanded, and they cut out large stones, costly stones, to lay the foundation of the house with worked stone.
verses.explainer: The king commands quarrying great, costly stones for the temple's foundation. The stones are cut and dressed at the quarry to be assembled silently at the building site.

verses.text: Solomon’s builders and Hiram’s builders and the Gebalites cut them, and prepared the timber and the stones to build the house.
verses.explainer: Solomon's and Hiram's builders and the Gebalites cut and prepare the timber and stones. International collaboration produces the materials for God's house.

annotations.word: the Gebalites

explainer.summary: The construction of Solomon's temple is described in detail: its dimensions, materials, chambers, and the inner sanctuary (Holy of Holies) with its golden cherubim. The building takes seven years. God promises that if Solomon obeys, He will dwell among Israel and never forsake them.
themes: The dwelling of God among humans
Obedience as the condition for divine presence
explainer.contextInBook: The temple description is the architectural centerpiece of 1 Kings. Every detail carries theological significance: the dimensions, the gold overlay, the cherubim, the windowless inner sanctuary. This is the place where heaven and earth intersect, where God's name dwells among His people.

wordStudies.translatedAs: The innermost room of the temple, the Holy of Holi
wordStudies.meaning: The innermost room of the temple, the Holy of Holies, where the ark rested. The word may come from a root meaning 'to speak' -- the devir is the place from which God speaks to His people.

wordStudies.translatedAs: No iron tools were used at the building site itsel
wordStudies.meaning: No iron tools were used at the building site itself. All stones were cut and dressed at the quarry. The silence at the construction site emphasized the sacred nature of the building -- no sound of human crafting profaned the holy ground.

wordStudies.translatedAs: The Holy of Holies, a perfect cube of twenty cubit
wordStudies.meaning: The Holy of Holies, a perfect cube of twenty cubits in each dimension. The cubic shape symbolizes perfection and completeness, matching the dimensions of the New Jerusalem in Revelation 21:16.

verses.text: In the four hundred and eightieth year after the children of Israel had come out of the land of Egypt, in the fourth year of Solomon’s reign over Israel, in the month Ziv, which is the second month, he began to build Yahweh’s house.
verses.explainer: Temple construction begins in the fourth year of Solomon's reign, the 480th year after the Exodus. The dating anchors the temple to Israel's founding salvation event.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: The 480 years may be either a literal chronological calculation or a symbolic number (12 generations x 40 years). Either way, it deliberately connects Solomon's temple to the Exodus, making the temple the destination of Israel's long journey from slavery to worship.

verses.text: The house which king Solomon built for Yahweh had a length of sixty cubits, and its width twenty, and its height thirty cubits.
verses.explainer: The temple is sixty cubits long, twenty wide, and thirty high (approximately 90 x 30 x 45 feet). It is modestly sized by modern standards but extraordinary for its time.

verses.text: The porch in front of the temple of the house had a length of twenty cubits, which was along the width of the house. Ten cubits was its width in front of the house.
verses.explainer: The portico before the main hall is twenty cubits wide and ten cubits deep. This entry hall creates a transitional space between the outside world and the holy interior.

verses.text: He made windows of fixed lattice work for the house.
verses.explainer: Windows with recessed frames provide light to the temple. These were likely clerestory windows high in the walls.

annotations.word: windows of fixed lattice

verses.text: Against the wall of the house, he built floors all around, against the walls of the house all around, both of the temple and of the inner sanctuary; and he made side rooms all around.
verses.explainer: Against the outer walls, Solomon builds three stories of side chambers used for storage and priestly functions.

verses.text: The lowest floor was five cubits wide, and the middle was six cubits wide, and the third was seven cubits wide; for on the outside he made offsets in the wall of the house all around, that the beams should not be inserted into the walls of the house.
verses.explainer: The three stories are of increasing width as they rise: five, six, and seven cubits. The walls narrow with each level, creating ledges for the floor beams.

verses.text: The house, when it was under construction, was built of stone prepared at the quarry; and no hammer or ax or any tool of iron was heard in the house while it was under construction.
verses.explainer: The building is constructed with stones fully prepared at the quarry -- no hammer, axe, or iron tool is heard at the construction site. Sacred silence marks the building process.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: The prohibition of iron tools at the sacred site echoes the altar law of Exodus 20:25, where hewn stones were forbidden for altars. Iron was associated with war and violence; its absence from the temple site emphasized peace and holiness.

crossReferences.connection: The altar law forbidding hewn stones -- the same principle of keeping tools of violence away from sacred spaces

verses.text: The door for the middle side rooms was in the right side of the house. They went up by winding stairs into the middle floor, and out of the middle into the third.
verses.explainer: The entrance to the side chambers is on the south side, with winding stairs connecting the three levels.

annotations.word: winding stairs

verses.text: So he built the house, and finished it; and he covered the house with beams and planks of cedar.
verses.explainer: Solomon finishes building the temple and covers it with beams and planks of cedar. The entire structure is enclosed in the finest available wood.

annotations.word: covered the house with beams and planks of cedar

verses.text: He built the floors all along the house, each five cubits high; and they rested on the house with timber of cedar.
verses.explainer: The side chambers are each five cubits high and attached to the temple with timbers of cedar. The entire complex is integrated into a unified structure.

verses.text: Yahweh’s word came to Solomon, saying,
verses.explainer: God speaks to Solomon during the construction with a conditional promise: obedience will bring divine presence.

verses.text: “Concerning this house which you are building, if you will walk in my statutes, and execute my ordinances, and keep all my commandments to walk in them; then I will establish my word with you, which I spoke to David your father.
verses.explainer: God conditions His presence on Solomon's obedience: walk in my statutes, execute my ordinances, keep my commandments. The temple is useless without covenant faithfulness.

annotations.word: if you will walk in my statutes

thenVsNow.then: God's interruption of the construction with a call to obedience makes the essential point: the most magnificent building is worthless without a faithful heart.
thenVsNow.now: God's interruption of the construction with a call to obedience makes the essential point: the most magnificent building is worthless without a faithful heart.

verses.text: I will dwell among the children of Israel, and will not forsake my people Israel.”
verses.explainer: God promises to dwell among Israel and never forsake them -- if the condition of obedience is met. The promise is the purpose behind the building.

annotations.word: I will dwell among the children of Israel, and will not forsake my people Israel

verses.text: So Solomon built the house, and finished it.
verses.explainer: Solomon completes the temple construction. The structural work is finished, and the detailed interior work begins.

annotations.word: So Solomon built the house, and finished it

verses.text: He built the walls of the house within with boards of cedar: from the floor of the house to the walls of the ceiling, he covered them on the inside with wood; and he covered the floor of the house with cypress boards.
verses.explainer: The interior walls are lined with cedar from floor to ceiling, and the floor is covered with cypress planks. No stone is visible inside.

verses.text: He built twenty cubits on the back part of the house with boards of cedar from the floor to the ceiling. He built them for it within, for an inner sanctuary, even for the most holy place.
verses.explainer: The inner twenty cubits of the temple are partitioned off as the inner sanctuary (Holy of Holies), lined floor to ceiling with cedar.

verses.text: In front of the temple sanctuary was forty cubits.
verses.explainer: The main hall before the inner sanctuary is forty cubits long. This is the holy place where priests conduct daily worship.

verses.text: There was cedar on the house within, carved with buds and open flowers. All was cedar. No stone was visible.
verses.explainer: The cedar of the interior is carved with gourds and open flowers. No stone is visible -- the entire interior is living, organic imagery.

verses.text: He prepared an inner sanctuary in the middle of the house within, to set the ark of Yahweh’s covenant there.
verses.explainer: The inner sanctuary is prepared to receive the ark of the covenant. This is the purpose of the entire building: housing God's covenant presence.

verses.text: Within the inner sanctuary was twenty cubits in length, and twenty cubits in width, and twenty cubits in its height; and he overlaid it with pure gold; and he covered the altar with cedar.
verses.explainer: The inner sanctuary is a perfect cube: twenty cubits in each dimension. It is entirely overlaid with pure gold -- walls, ceiling, and floor.

annotations.word: he overlaid it with pure gold

crossReferences.connection: The New Jerusalem is also a perfect cube -- the Holy of Holies' shape foreshadows the ultimate dwelling of God with humanity

verses.text: So Solomon overlaid the house within with pure gold. He drew chains of gold across before the inner sanctuary, and he overlaid it with gold.
verses.explainer: Solomon overlays the interior of the main hall with gold and draws chains of gold across the front of the inner sanctuary.

annotations.word: chains of gold across before the inner sanctuary

verses.text: He overlaid the whole house with gold, until all the house was finished. He also overlaid the whole altar that belonged to the inner sanctuary with gold.
verses.explainer: The entire temple interior is overlaid with gold, including the altar belonging to the inner sanctuary. Gold covers everything visible.

verses.text: In the inner sanctuary he made two cherubim of olive wood, each ten cubits high.
verses.explainer: Two cherubim of olive wood are made for the inner sanctuary, each ten cubits (fifteen feet) tall. These massive figures guard the ark.

annotations.word: two cherubim of olive wood, each ten cubits high

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: Cherubim in the ancient Near East were not the chubby babies of Renaissance art but massive, composite guardian figures combining features of different creatures. They represented divine protection and the boundary between the earthly and heavenly realms.

verses.text: Five cubits was the one wing of the cherub, and five cubits the other wing of the cherub. From the tip of one wing to the tip of the other was ten cubits.
verses.explainer: Each cherub has a wingspan of ten cubits (fifteen feet). The wings of both cherubim together span the entire twenty-cubit width of the inner sanctuary.

verses.text: The other cherub was ten cubits. Both the cherubim were of one measure and one form.
verses.explainer: Both cherubim are identical in size and form -- symmetrical guardians flanking the ark.

verses.text: One cherub was ten cubits high, and so was the other cherub.
verses.explainer: Each cherub stands ten cubits tall. The scale emphasizes the grandeur of the inner sanctuary.

verses.text: He set the cherubim within the inner house. The wings of the cherubim were stretched out, so that the wing of the one touched the one wall, and the wing of the other cherub touched the other wall; and their wings touched one another in the middle of the house.
verses.explainer: The cherubim are placed so their wings touch in the center and reach to both walls. The ark sits beneath this canopy of wings.

annotations.word: their wings touched one another in the middle of the house

verses.text: He overlaid the cherubim with gold.
verses.explainer: The cherubim are overlaid with gold, matching the gold-covered walls, floor, and ceiling. The entire room is gold.

verses.text: He carved all the walls of the house around with carved figures of cherubim, palm trees, and open flowers, inside and outside.
verses.explainer: All the walls of the temple, inner and outer rooms, are carved with cherubim, palm trees, and flowers -- garden imagery throughout.

crossReferences.connection: Cherubim were placed to guard the way to the tree of life after the expulsion from Eden. The temple's cherubim imagery suggests a return to God's presence

verses.text: He overlaid the floor of the house with gold, inside and outside.
verses.explainer: The floor of both the inner and outer rooms is overlaid with gold. Every surface in the temple gleams.

verses.text: For the entrance of the inner sanctuary, he made doors of olive wood. The lintel and door posts were a fifth part of the wall.
verses.explainer: The entrance to the inner sanctuary has doors of olive wood with a five-sided doorframe. The doors are the boundary between the Holy Place and the Holy of Holies.

verses.explainer: The olive wood doors are carved with cherubim, palm trees, and flowers, and overlaid with gold. Even the doors are works of art.

verses.text: He carved cherubim, palm trees, and open flowers; and he overlaid them with gold fitted on the engraved work.

explainer.summary: Solomon builds his own palace complex over thirteen years (compared to seven for the temple). The chapter then describes Hiram of Tyre's bronze work for the temple: the two great pillars Jachin and Boaz, the bronze sea, the ten stands with lavers, and all the golden furnishings.
themes: The symbolic meaning of temple furnishings
The completion of the physical preparations for worship
explainer.contextInBook: The contrast between seven years for the temple and thirteen for the palace is noted but not moralized by the narrator. The detailed description of temple furnishings emphasizes that every object in God's house is crafted with the highest skill and finest materials.

wordStudies.translatedAs: The two bronze pillars flanking the temple entranc
wordStudies.meaning: The two bronze pillars flanking the temple entrance. Jachin means 'He establishes' and Boaz means 'In Him is strength.' Together they declare that God establishes His house and His strength sustains it.

wordStudies.translatedAs: A massive bronze basin for priestly washing, holdi
wordStudies.meaning: A massive bronze basin for priestly washing, holding about 12,000 gallons. Called a 'sea' because of its enormous size, it symbolized the primordial waters over which God exercised sovereignty.

verses.text: Solomon was building his own house thirteen years, and he finished all his house.
verses.explainer: Solomon spends thirteen years building his own palace -- nearly double the seven years for the temple. The narrator notes this without explicit commentary.

verses.text: It was covered with cedar above over the forty-five beams, that were on the pillars, fifteen in a row.

verses.text: There were beams in three rows, and window was facing window in three ranks.

verses.text: The foundation was of costly stones, even great stones, stones of ten cubits, and stones of eight cubits.

thenVsNow.then: The temple's entrance declares its theology: 'He establishes' and 'In Him is strength.' Before you even enter, the pillars announce that everything within depends on God, not human achievement.

verses.text: He made the molten sea of ten cubits from brim to brim, round in shape. Its height was five cubits; and a line of thirty cubits encircled it.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: The bronze sea served the practical function of priestly purification but its name and symbolism go deeper: the 'sea' represented the chaos waters that God conquered at creation. Placing a tamed 'sea' in the temple courtyard declared God's sovereignty over the forces of chaos.

verses.text: Under its brim around there were buds which encircled it for ten cubits, encircling the sea. The buds were in two rows, cast when it was cast.
verses.explainer: Below the rim, gourds encircle the sea in two rows, ten per cubit, cast as part of the basin.

verses.text: It stood on twelve oxen, three looking toward the north, and three looking toward the west, and three looking toward the south, and three looking toward the east; and the sea was set on them above, and all their hindquarters were inward.
verses.explainer: The sea stands on twelve bronze oxen: three facing each compass direction. The oxen represent the twelve tribes supporting the worship center.

verses.text: It was a hand width thick. Its brim was worked like the brim of a cup, like the flower of a lily. It held two thousand baths.
verses.explainer: The sea's walls are a handbreadth thick. It holds two thousand baths (about 12,000 gallons). Its rim is shaped like a lily blossom.

verses.text: He made the ten bases of brass. The length of one base was four cubits, four cubits its width, and three cubits its height.
verses.explainer: Hiram makes ten bronze stands, each four cubits long, four wide, and three high. These portable bases carry the lavers for washing sacrificial parts.

verses.text: The work of the bases was like this: they had panels; and there were panels between the ledges;
verses.explainer: The stands have panels set between uprights, creating framed surfaces for decorative work.

verses.text: and on the panels that were between the ledges were lions, oxen, and cherubim; and on the ledges there was a pedestal above; and beneath the lions and oxen were wreaths of hanging work.

verses.explainer: Each stand has four bronze wheels with bronze axles and four supports for the basin at the corners.

verses.text: Its mouth within the capital and above was a cubit. Its mouth was round after the work of a pedestal, a cubit and a half; and also on its mouth were engravings, and their panels were square, not round.
verses.explainer: The basin opening is round, set in a frame a cubit and a half across, with engravings on the panels.

verses.text: The four wheels were underneath the panels; and the axles of the wheels were in the base. The height of a wheel was a cubit and half a cubit.
verses.explainer: The four wheels are underneath the panels, attached with axles to the base. Each wheel is a cubit and a half in diameter.

verses.text: The work of the wheels was like the work of a chariot wheel. Their axles, and their rims, and their spokes, and their naves, were all of cast metal.
verses.explainer: The wheels are made like chariot wheels -- the same technology that drives Israel's military now serves the temple's worship needs.

verses.text: There were four supports at the four corners of each base. Its supports were of the base itself.
verses.explainer: Four supports at the corners of each stand extend from the base itself, providing structural integrity for the heavy basin above.

verses.text: In the top of the base there was a round band half a cubit high; and on the top of the base its supports and its panels were of the same.
verses.explainer: The top of each stand has a round band half a cubit high, with handles and panels cast as part of it.

verses.text: On the plates of its supports, and on its panels, he engraved cherubim, lions, and palm trees, each in its space, with wreaths all around.
verses.explainer: The surfaces are engraved with cherubim, lions, and palm trees wherever there is space, with wreaths around them.

verses.explainer: Five stands are placed on the south side of the temple and five on the north, with the bronze sea at the southeast corner.

verses.explainer: Hiram also makes the pots, shovels, and basins. The list of finished work summarizes his complete contribution.

verses.text: the two pillars; the two bowls of the capitals that were on the top of the pillars; the two networks to cover the two bowls of the capitals that were on the top of the pillars;

explainer.summary: Solomon dedicates the temple in a grand ceremony. The ark is brought into the Holy of Holies, and God's glory fills the temple as a cloud. Solomon delivers a dedication address, then offers an extensive prayer covering seven scenarios in which Israel might turn to God in the temple. He concludes with sacrifices and a fourteen-day feast.
themes: The temple as a place of mercy for all peoples
explainer.contextInBook: This is the theological climax of 1 Kings and arguably of the entire Deuteronomic history. Solomon's prayer establishes the temple as the place where God hears prayer, forgives sin, and restores relationship. It becomes the foundation for all subsequent Jewish theology of prayer and repentance.

wordStudies.translatedAs: The cloud (anan) that fills the temple is the same
wordStudies.meaning: The cloud (anan) that fills the temple is the same glory cloud that led Israel through the wilderness and descended on Mount Sinai. It represents God's visible, tangible presence -- too intense for human habitation.

wordStudies.translatedAs: The highest heaven -- Solomon acknowledges that ev
wordStudies.meaning: The highest heaven -- Solomon acknowledges that even the universe cannot contain God, much less a building. The temple does not house God but provides a place where humans can access God's attentive presence.

wordStudies.translatedAs: The repeated refrain of Solomon's prayer: 'hear in
wordStudies.meaning: The repeated refrain of Solomon's prayer: 'hear in heaven your dwelling place, and when you hear, forgive.' Prayer on earth reaches heaven because the temple serves as a communication channel between the two realms.

verses.text: Then Solomon assembled the elders of Israel, with all the heads of the tribes, the princes of the fathers’ households of the children of Israel, to king Solomon in Jerusalem, to bring up the ark of Yahweh’s covenant out of David’s city, which is Zion.

culturalNotes.note: The Feast of Tabernacles (Sukkot) was a seven-day harvest celebration during which Israel lived in booths, remembering their wilderness wanderings. Dedicating the temple during this feast connected God's past provision in the wilderness with His future dwelling in the temple.

explainer.summary: Solomon's foreign wives turn his heart to other gods. God raises adversaries: Hadad the Edomite, Rezon of Damascus, and Jeroboam the Ephraimite. explainer.contextInBook: This is the most devastating chapter in 1 Kings. The wisest man alive falls into the most basic sin: worshiping other gods. explainer.summary: Rehoboam goes to Shechem where the northern tribes demand relief from Solomon's heavy labor and taxes. He rejects the elders' wise counsel and follows the young men's advice, harshly refusing any relief. The ten northern tribes secede under Jeroboam. Jeroboam establishes golden calf worship at Dan and Bethel to prevent his people from returning to Jerusalem.

explainer.contextInBook: This is the moment the kingdom fractures -- the consequence of Solomon's sin prophesied in chapter 11. Rehoboam's foolishness provides the occasion, but the real cause is Solomon's apostasy. Jeroboam's golden calves then establish the sin pattern that will destroy the northern kingdom.

verses.text: When all Israel heard that Jeroboam had returned, they sent and called him to the congregation, and made him king over all Israel. There was no one who followed David’s house, except for the tribe of Judah only.
verses.explainer: All Israel makes Jeroboam king. Only the tribe of Judah remains with the house of David.

verses.text: When Rehoboam had come to Jerusalem, he assembled all the house of Judah and the tribe of Benjamin, a hundred and eighty thousand chosen men, who were warriors, to fight against the house of Israel, to bring the kingdom again to Rehoboam the son of Solomon.
verses.explainer: Rehoboam assembles 180,000 warriors from Judah and Benjamin to reconquer the north by force.

verses.text: But the word of God came to Shemaiah the man of God, saying,
verses.explainer: God sends the prophet Shemaiah to forbid the war: this thing is from Me. Rehoboam must not fight against his brothers.

verses.text: “Speak to Rehoboam the son of Solomon, king of Judah, and to all the house of Judah and Benjamin, and to the rest of the people, saying,
verses.explainer: Shemaiah declares God's word: do not go up or fight against your brothers the children of Israel. Every man go home.

verses.text: ‘Yahweh says, “You shall not go up or fight against your brothers, the children of Israel. Everyone return to his house; for this thing is from me.”’” So they listened to Yahweh’s word, and returned and went their way, according to Yahweh’s word.
verses.explainer: They obey Shemaiah's word and return home. The army disperses, and the kingdom remains divided.

verses.text: Then Jeroboam built Shechem in the hill country of Ephraim, and lived in it; and he went out from there, and built Penuel.
verses.explainer: Jeroboam fortifies Shechem in Ephraim as his capital and also builds Penuel east of the Jordan. He establishes the northern kingdom's infrastructure.

verses.text: Jeroboam said in his heart, “Now the kingdom will return to David’s house.
verses.explainer: Jeroboam's fatal reasoning: if the people go up to sacrifice at the Jerusalem temple, their hearts will return to Rehoboam.

verses.text: If this people goes up to offer sacrifices in Yahweh’s house at Jerusalem, then the heart of this people will turn again to their lord, even to Rehoboam king of Judah; and they will kill me, and return to Rehoboam king of Judah.”
verses.explainer: Jeroboam fears the people will kill him and return to the Davidic dynasty if they continue worshiping in Jerusalem.

annotations.word: they will kill me, and return to Rehoboam king of Judah

verses.text: So the king took counsel, and made two calves of gold; and he said to them, “It is too much for you to go up to Jerusalem. Look and behold your gods, Israel, which brought you up out of the land of Egypt!”
verses.explainer: Jeroboam's solution: two golden calves placed at Dan and Bethel. He tells the people: 'These are your gods who brought you out of Egypt!'

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: Jeroboam may have intended the calves as pedestals for Yahweh (like the cherubim in the temple) rather than as representations of other gods. But the visual similarity to Canaanite Baal worship made the distinction meaningless in practice. The calves became objects of worship themselves.

crossReferences.connection: Aaron's golden calf at Sinai used the identical formula: 'These are your gods, O Israel, who brought you up out of Egypt.' Jeroboam consciously repeats the original sin

verses.text: He set the one in Bethel, and the other put he in Dan.
verses.explainer: One calf is placed in Bethel (southern border of the northern kingdom) and one in Dan (northern border). The entire territory is covered.

verses.text: This thing became a sin; for the people went even as far as Dan to worship before the one there.
verses.explainer: This thing becomes a sin. The people go before the one at Dan. Jeroboam's political strategy creates permanent apostasy.

verses.text: He made houses of high places, and made priests from among all the people, who were not of the sons of Levi.
verses.explainer: Jeroboam makes houses on high places and appoints priests from the general population who are not Levites. He creates a rival priesthood.

annotations.word: made priests from among all the people, who were not of the sons of Levi

verses.text: Jeroboam ordained a feast in the eighth month, on the fifteenth day of the month, like the feast that is in Judah, and he went up to the altar. He did so in Bethel, sacrificing to the calves that he had made, and he placed in Bethel the priests of the high places that he had made.
verses.explainer: Jeroboam institutes a festival in the eighth month to rival Judah's seventh-month Feast of Tabernacles. He personally offers sacrifice.

annotations.word: Jeroboam ordained a feast in the eighth month

verses.text: He went up to the altar which he had made in Bethel on the fifteenth day in the eighth month, even in the month which he had devised of his own heart; and he ordained a feast for the children of Israel, and went up to the altar, to burn incense.
verses.explainer: Jeroboam offers on the altar he made at Bethel, burning incense. He establishes the feast he invented from his own heart.

annotations.word: which he had devised of his own heart

explainer.summary: A man of God from Judah prophesies against Jeroboam's altar at Bethel, predicting that King Josiah will one day desecrate it. Jeroboam's hand withers when he orders the prophet's arrest, then is restored when he asks for prayer. The man of God is tricked by an old prophet into disobeying God's command, and a lion kills him on the road.
themes: The danger of disobedience even for prophets
explainer.contextInBook: The unnamed prophet's message against Bethel's altar previews its destruction by Josiah three hundred years later (2 Kings 23:15-16). His own death for disobedience warns that no one -- not even prophets -- is exempt from the consequences of violating God's specific commands.

wordStudies.translatedAs: A prophetic title indicating divine authorization
wordStudies.meaning: A prophetic title indicating divine authorization and commissioning. The unnamed prophet from Judah carries God's authority to confront royal sin, even in foreign territory.

wordStudies.translatedAs: The prophet names a specific future king by name,
wordStudies.meaning: The prophet names a specific future king by name, three hundred years before his birth. This extraordinary specificity of prophecy is unique in the Old Testament and demonstrates divine foreknowledge.

verses.text: Behold, a man of God came out of Judah by Yahweh’s word to Beth El; and Jeroboam was standing by the altar to burn incense.
verses.explainer: A man of God arrives from Judah by God's command, confronting Jeroboam at the Bethel altar. Prophetic authority challenges royal authority.

verses.text: He cried against the altar by Yahweh’s word, and said, “Altar! Altar! Yahweh says: ‘Behold, a son will be born to David’s house, Josiah by name. On you he will sacrifice the priests of the high places who burn incense on you, and they will burn men’s bones on you.’”
verses.explainer: The prophet cries against the altar: 'A child named Josiah will burn the bones of your priests on you.' A prophecy three hundred years in advance.

annotations.word: Josiah by name

crossReferences.connection: Josiah destroys the Bethel altar and burns human bones on it in 622 BC -- the precise fulfillment of this prophecy from three centuries earlier

verses.text: He gave a sign the same day, saying, “This is the sign which Yahweh has spoken: Behold, the altar will be split apart, and the ashes that are on it will be poured out.”
verses.explainer: The prophet gives a sign: the altar will split apart and its ashes will pour out. The immediate sign validates the long-term prophecy.

verses.text: When the king heard the saying of the man of God, which he cried against the altar in Bethel, Jeroboam put out his hand from the altar, saying, “Seize him!” His hand, which he put out against him, dried up, so that he could not draw it back again to himself.
verses.explainer: Jeroboam stretches out his hand and orders 'Seize him!' but his hand withers and he cannot draw it back. Royal power is instantly paralyzed.

verses.text: The altar was also split apart, and the ashes poured out from the altar, according to the sign which the man of God had given by Yahweh’s word.
verses.explainer: The altar splits apart and the ashes pour out, fulfilling the sign. The immediate prophecy is confirmed.

verses.text:
The king answered the man of God, “Now intercede for the favor of Yahweh your God, and pray for me, that my hand may be restored me again.”
The man of God interceded with Yahweh, and the king’s hand was restored to him again, and became as it was before.
verses.explainer: Jeroboam begs the prophet to pray for his hand's restoration. The king who tried to silence the prophet now depends on him.

verses.text: The king said to the man of God, “Come home with me, and refresh yourself, and I will give you a reward.”
verses.explainer: The king invites the prophet home for a meal and a reward, but the prophet refuses -- God commanded him not to eat, drink, or return the same way.

annotations.word: Come home with me, and refresh yourself, and I will give you a reward

verses.text: The man of God said to the king, “Even if you gave me half of your house, I would not go in with you, neither would I eat bread nor drink water in this place;
verses.explainer: The prophet refuses absolutely: even if Jeroboam gave him half his house, he would not eat or drink or return the same way.

verses.text: for so was it commanded me by Yahweh’s word, saying, ‘You shall eat no bread, drink no water, and don’t return by the way that you came.’”
verses.explainer: God's explicit command: do not eat bread, drink water, or return by the way you came. Three specific prohibitions.

verses.text: So he went another way, and didn’t return by the way that he came to Bethel.
verses.explainer: The prophet leaves by a different route, obeying God's command. So far, his obedience is exemplary.

verses.text: Now an old prophet lived in Bethel, and one of his sons came and told him all the works that the man of God had done that day in Bethel. They also told their father the words which he had spoken to the king.
verses.explainer: An old prophet living in Bethel hears from his sons what happened. He asks which way the man of God went and goes after him.

annotations.word: an old prophet lived in Bethel

verses.text: Their father said to them, “Which way did he go?” Now his sons had seen which way the man of God went, who came from Judah.
verses.explainer: The old prophet's sons show him which direction the man of God took. He saddles his donkey and pursues.

annotations.word: his sons had seen which way the man of God went

verses.text: He said to his sons, “Saddle the donkey for me.” So they saddled the donkey for him; and he rode on it.
verses.explainer: The old prophet follows the man of God and finds him sitting under an oak. His rest under the tree creates vulnerability to temptation.

verses.text:
He went after the man of God, and found him sitting under an oak. He said to him, “Are you the man of God who came from Judah?”
He said, “I am.”
verses.explainer: The old prophet asks: are you the man of God from Judah? The identification confirms the target.

annotations.word: Are you the man of God who came from Judah?

verses.text: Then he said to him, “Come home with me, and eat bread.”
verses.explainer: The old prophet invites him home to eat. A seemingly hospitable invitation that will prove fatal.

annotations.word: Come home with me, and eat bread

verses.text: He said, “I may not return with you, nor go in with you. I will not eat bread or drink water with you in this place.
verses.explainer: The man of God refuses, repeating God's explicit prohibition: I cannot return with you, eat bread, or drink water in this place.

annotations.word: I may not return with you, nor go in with you

verses.text: For it was said to me by Yahweh’s word, ‘You shall eat no bread or drink water there, and don’t turn again to go by the way that you came.’”
verses.explainer: He quotes God's command exactly: eat no bread, drink no water, do not return the way you came.

verses.text: He said to him, “I also am a prophet as you are; and an angel spoke to me by Yahweh’s word, saying, ‘Bring him back with you into your house, that he may eat bread and drink water.’” He lied to him.
verses.explainer: The old prophet lies: 'An angel spoke to me by God's word saying bring him back.' The old prophet deceives his colleague with a false prophetic claim.

verses.text: So he went back with him, ate bread in his house, and drank water.
verses.explainer: The man of God goes back, eats, and drinks with the old prophet. He obeys a human claim over God's explicit command.

thenVsNow.then: The man of God who resisted a king's pressure succumbs to a fellow prophet's lie. This warns that spiritual danger often comes not from obvious enemies but from those who look like allies.
thenVsNow.now: The man of God who resisted a king's pressure succumbs to a fellow prophet's lie. This warns that spiritual danger often comes not from obvious enemies but from those who look like allies.

verses.text: As they sat at the table, Yahweh’s word came to the prophet who brought him back;
verses.explainer: While eating, the word of Yahweh comes to the old prophet -- the man who lied now delivers genuine prophecy against the man he deceived.

verses.text: and he cried out to the man of God who came from Judah, saying, “Yahweh says, ‘Because you have been disobedient to Yahweh’s mouth, and have not kept the commandment which Yahweh your God commanded you,
verses.explainer: The old prophet announces judgment: because you disobeyed God's command, your body will not come to your fathers' tomb.

verses.text: but came back, and have eaten bread and drank water in the place of which he said to you, “Eat no bread, and drink no water”; your body will not come to the tomb of your fathers.’”
verses.explainer: The prophetic word explains: because you ate and drank in the place God forbade, you will not be buried in your family tomb.

verses.text: After he had eaten bread, and after he drank, he saddled the donkey for the prophet whom he had brought back.
verses.explainer: After the meal, the old prophet saddles a donkey for the man of God. The hospitality continues even after the death sentence is pronounced.

verses.text: When he had gone, a lion met him by the way and killed him. His body was thrown on the path, and the donkey stood by it. The lion also stood by the body.
verses.explainer: A lion meets the man of God on the road and kills him. The lion stands beside the body with the donkey -- neither eating the corpse nor attacking the donkey.

verses.text: Behold, men passed by, and saw the body thrown on the path, and the lion standing by the body; and they came and told it in the city where the old prophet lived.
verses.explainer: Passersby see the body and the lion standing beside it and report this in Bethel, where the old prophet lives.

annotations.word: the lion standing by the body

verses.text: When the prophet who brought him back from the way heard of it, he said, “It is the man of God who was disobedient to Yahweh’s mouth. Therefore Yahweh has delivered him to the lion, which has mauled him and slain him, according to Yahweh’s word, which he spoke to him.”
verses.explainer: The old prophet hears the news and recognizes it as the fulfillment of the word he himself delivered: the man of God disobeyed, and God's lion killed him.

verses.text: He said to his sons, saying, “Saddle the donkey for me,” and they saddled it.
verses.explainer: The old prophet retrieves the body, mourning: 'Alas, my brother!' Genuine grief mixed with guilt for his role in the tragedy.

verses.text: He went and found his body thrown on the path, and the donkey and the lion standing by the body. The lion had not eaten the body, nor mauled the donkey.
verses.explainer: He finds the body on the road with the donkey and lion still standing by it. The lion has not eaten the body or attacked the donkey.

verses.text: The prophet took up the body of the man of God, and laid it on the donkey, and brought it back. He came to the city of the old prophet to mourn, and to bury him.
verses.explainer: The old prophet retrieves the body, brings it back to Bethel, mourns over it, and buries it in his own tomb.

verses.text: He laid his body in his own grave; and they mourned over him, saying, “Alas, my brother!”
verses.explainer: They mourn him saying: 'Alas, my brother!' The community recognizes the loss of a genuine prophet.

verses.text: After he had buried him, he spoke to his sons, saying, “When I am dead, bury me in the tomb in which the man of God is buried. Lay my bones beside his bones.
verses.explainer: The old prophet instructs his sons: when I die, bury me in the same tomb. Lay my bones beside his bones.

annotations.word: Lay my bones beside his bones

verses.text: For the saying which he cried by Yahweh’s word against the altar in Bethel, and against all the houses of the high places which are in the cities of Samaria, will surely happen.”
verses.explainer: The old prophet affirms that the prophecy against Bethel's altar and the high places of Samaria will surely come true.

verses.text: After this thing Jeroboam didn’t return from his evil way, but again made priests of the high places from among all the people. Whoever wanted to, he consecrated him, that there might be priests of the high places.
verses.explainer: Jeroboam does not turn from his evil way. He continues appointing non-Levitical priests from all the people. The prophetic warning changes nothing.

verses.text: This thing became sin to the house of Jeroboam, even to cut it off, and to destroy it from off the surface of the earth.
verses.explainer: This became the sin of Jeroboam's house, leading to its destruction and extinction from the face of the earth.

explainer.summary: Jeroboam's son falls ill. He sends his wife in disguise to Ahijah the prophet, who sees through the disguise and pronounces judgment: the child will die, and Jeroboam's dynasty will be destroyed. The child dies as prophesied. The chapter also covers Rehoboam's reign in Judah, marked by idolatry and Shishak's invasion.
themes: The inadequacy of disguises before God
The parallel decline of both kingdoms
The beginning of the cyclical pattern
explainer.contextInBook: This chapter concludes the Jeroboam narrative and begins the alternating pattern of northern and southern king evaluations that structures the rest of 1-2 Kings. Both kingdoms are already in spiritual decline within a generation of Solomon.

wordStudies.translatedAs: Jeroboam tells his wife to disguise herself -- the
wordStudies.meaning: Jeroboam tells his wife to disguise herself -- the same impulse that drove his entire religious policy: substitution and deception. He cannot face truth directly, so he sends a disguised messenger to a prophet who sees all.

wordStudies.translatedAs: Pharaoh Shishak (Shoshenq I) invaded Judah around
wordStudies.meaning: Pharaoh Shishak (Shoshenq I) invaded Judah around 926 BC. His campaign is confirmed by Egyptian records and his victory stela at Karnak. This is one of the earliest biblical events confirmed by external archaeology.

verses.text: At that time Abijah the son of Jeroboam became sick.
verses.explainer: Jeroboam's son Abijah falls sick. The king seeks prophetic guidance about the child's fate.

verses.text: Jeroboam said to his wife, “Please get up and disguise yourself, so that you won’t be recognized as Jeroboam’s wife. Go to Shiloh. Behold, Ahijah the prophet is there, who said that that I would be king over this people.
verses.explainer: Jeroboam sends his wife in disguise to Ahijah the prophet at Shiloh. He fears being recognized as the king who rejected Ahijah's God.

verses.text: Take with you ten loaves of bread, some cakes, and a jar of honey, and go to him. He will tell you what will become of the child.”
